---
title: "Paper 32: Is there a fair allocation of healthcare research funds by the European Union?"
author: Lee Jones \- Senior Biostatistician \- Statistical Review
date: '`r format(Sys.Date(), "%B %d, %Y")`'

filters:
  - minus_html.lua

format:
  html:
    smart: false
    embed-resources: true
    theme: journal
    toc: true
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, warning=FALSE, message = FALSE)

```

```{css}
a { color: blue !important;}
h1 {font-size: 22px !important;}
```

```{r, results='hide'}
lapply(c("figures", "data"), function(d) if (!dir.exists(d)) dir.create(d, recursive = TRUE))
```

```{r}
normalQ<- "QQ-plot looks roughly normal."
notnormalQ<- "QQ-plot indicates the residuals are not normally distributed."
vifprob<-"Overall, when taking into account VIF and SE, the model has collinearity problems."
vifnoprob<-"Overall, when taking into account VIF and SE, the model does not have collinearity issues."
indDesNo<-"The study design is not independent and should be assessed using linear mixed models or generalized estimating equations."
indDesYes<-"The study design seems to be independent."
homYes<-"There is no distinct funnelling pattern observed, supporting homoscedasticity of residuals."
homNo<-"The residuals vs. fitted plot is heteroscedastic with funnelling shape."
somehet<-"Some heteroscedasticity is present in plots, and a sensitivity analysis using weighted or robust regression or wild bootstrapping is recommended."
Cooksnoproblem<-"Cook's Distance and other influence diagnostics indicated no observations of concern."
linyes<-"No linearity violation was observed in either plots or tests."
linno<-"Plots and tests show an unaccounted non-linear relationship may be present."
linplotno<-"Plots show an unaccounted non-linear relationship may be present."
lintestno<-"Tests show an unaccounted non-linear relationship may be present."
linout<-"Linearity issues are likely due to outliers within the data."
p_repro<-"The p-values in this model were reproduced with mean differences for p-values close to zero."
OutliersYes <-"Outliers present with high Cook's distance values indicating a sensitivity analysis should be performed to assess the change in the regression coefficients."
MostCompRep<- "This model was mostly computationally reproducible, with minor rounding errors. P-values were reproduced and had the same interpretation, and regression coefficients did not change direction."
CompRep<-"This model was computationally reproducible, with all reported statistics that were assessed being reproducible."
NotCompRep<-"This model was not computationally reproducible."
 Nosens<-"As this model was not computationally reproducible, inferential reproducibility was not considered, since the original analyses could not be reproduced and therefore, statistical assumptions could not be meaningfully compared or interpreted."

evalAump<-"Evaluate the assumptions of the linear regression models by examining residuals, identifying influential outliers, and assessing multicollinearity among predictors. If any assumptions are violated, address them using appropriate methods."
use_VIF<-"Formally assess multicollinearity using variance inflation factors (VIFs) and examination of coefficient standard errors."
uniqueID <- "Include a unique person identifier (ID) in the dataset to ensure each individual can be distinctly identified."
Scat<-"Consider using Scatterplots to visualise the relationships between variables."
Box<-"Consider using box-plots to visualise the data."
AdjR2<- "Adjusted R^2^ should be reported for multivariable models."
datD<- "Include a data dictionary."
Includedata<- "Include data to reproduce all analyses in the paper and supplementary files."
Fullydescribe<- "Fully describe the model used in the linear regression."
DetailAssump<-"Provide details of linear regression assumption checks."
Code<- "Consider creating a reproducible analysis workflow and sharing the code."
Update_dataAvail<-"Update the data availability statement to reflect the status of the data."
coef95<-"Report linear regression coefficients and 95% confidence intervals."
Analysis<-"Provide tables in the Supporting Information that present all analyses conducted in the paper, including full model outputs such as regression coefficients and all variables used for adjustment."
Missing<-"Provide a clear description of the amount and pattern of missing data, along with the methods used to address missingness in the analyses."
Modelling<-"Ensure that the modelling process is clearly described and applied consistently throughout the paper. The type of model and its key specifications should be included in the footnotes of the tables to improve transparency."
File_format<- "Data underlying statistical analyses should be provided in a standard data format (e.g., CSV, XLSX, or similar) so it is machine readable."
Data_format<-"Provide the data in a recognised long or wide format, with a unique participant identifier included to allow assessment of repeated measures and clustering."
Program<-"Specify the statistical software package, version, and any relevant options, procedures, or settings required to reproduce the analysis."
Stepwise<-"Ensure complete reporting of the stepwise model selection procedure, including the direction of selection (forward, backward, or bidirectional) and the criteria for variable entry and removal."
Multiple_test<-"Acknowledge the issue of multiple testing in the results and/or limitations section, including whether adjustments were applied or why none were considered necessary."
Effect_size<-"Place greater emphasis on the magnitude and practical relevance of regression coefficients, including their confidence intervals, rather than focusing predominantly on statistical significance."
```

```{r pack}
library(tidyverse)
library(emmeans)
library(haven)#spss
library(broom) # for regression tables
library(summarytools) # for tables
library (gtsummary) #for models 
library(tables) # for tables
library (flextable)
library(psych)
library(MASS)
library (leaps)
library(lmboot)
library(car)
library(jtools)
library(lmtest)
library(sandwich)
library(httr)
library(readxl)
library(jmv)
library(pillar)
library(htmltools)
library(gridExtra)
library(blandr)
library(rvest)
library(effects)
library(visreg)
library(forestplot)
library(conflicted)
library(httr)
library(forcats)
library(cowplot)
library(docxtractr)
library(tesseract)

# capture session information in a text file
sessionInfo() %>% capture.output(file="session_info.txt")


conflict_prefer("compose", "flextable") 
conflict_prefer("rename", "dplyr") 
conflict_prefer("filter", "dplyr") 
conflict_prefer("select", "dplyr") 
conflicts_prefer(stats::contr.treatment)

# capture session information in a text file
sessionInfo() %>% capture.output(file="session_info.txt")



#flextable global rounding
set_flextable_defaults(digits = 3)
otherRound<-3
dig<-3
thresround<-4

options(scipen = 999)

#GT summary theme
my_theme <-
  list(
    # round large p-values to two places
    "pkgwide-fn:pvalue_fun" = function(x) style_pvalue(x, digits = 3),
    "pkgwide-fn:prependpvalue_fun" = function(x) style_pvalue(x, digits = 3, prepend_p = TRUE),
    # report median (IQR) and n (percent) as default stats in `tbl_summary()`
    "tbl_summary-str:continuous_stat" = "{mean} ({SD})",
    "tbl_summary-str:categorical_stat" = "{n} ({p}%)") 
 
set_gtsummary_theme(my_theme)

flag_any <- function(df, term_name, condition) {
  df |>
    filter(term == term_name) |>
    summarise(flag = any({{ condition }}, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
    pull(flag)
}



Sround <- function(x, k) {
  trimws(format(round(x, k), nsmall = k, scientific = FALSE))
}



# Function to round to 4 decimal places
round_p <- function(x) ifelse(is.na(x), "", ifelse(x < 0.001, "<0.001", sprintf("%.4f", x)))


# Function to print summary about VIF values
printvif<- function(vif_values) {
  if (all(is.na(vif_values))) {
    print("All values are NA")
  } else if (any(!is.na(vif_values) & vif_values >= 5)) {
    print("Collinearity issues")
  } else if (any(!is.na(vif_values) & vif_values < 5 & vif_values >= 3)) {
    print("All VIF values are under five, indicating some colinearity but within reasonable bounds.")
  } else if (any(!is.na(vif_values) & vif_values < 3)) {
    print("All VIF values are under three, indicating no collinearity issues.")
  }
}

# Function to generate bullet points for each variable
generate_bullet_points <- function(data) {
  bullet_points <- data %>%
    rowwise() %>%
    mutate(text = paste0("* ", term, ": ", Condition)) %>%
    pull(text)
  
  return(bullet_points)
}


ols_plot_dfbetas_fixed_y <- function(model, print_plot = TRUE, fixed_ylim = TRUE) {
  olsrr:::check_model(model)
  
  dfb <- dfbetas(model)
  n <- nrow(dfb)
  np <- ncol(dfb)
  threshold <- 2 / sqrt(n)
  myplots <- list()
  outliers <- list()
  
  # Precompute y-axis range and expand upper limit if fixed_ylim is TRUE
  if (fixed_ylim) {
    y_range <- range(dfb, na.rm = TRUE)
    y_range[2] <- y_range[2] + 0.05 * diff(y_range)  # Add 5% buffer at top
  }

  for (i in seq_len(np)) {
    dbetas <- dfb[, i]
    df_data <- data.frame(obs = seq_len(n), dbetas = dbetas)
    d <- olsrr:::ols_prep_dfbeta_data(df_data, threshold)
    f <- olsrr:::ols_prep_dfbeta_outliers(d)

    # Determine where to place threshold label
    if (fixed_ylim) {
      y_text_pos <- y_range[2] - 0.01 * diff(y_range)  # Slightly below max
    } else {
      y_range_this <- range(d$dbetas, na.rm = TRUE)
      y_text_pos <- y_range_this[2] + 0.05 * diff(y_range_this)
    }

    p <- ggplot(d, aes(x = obs, y = dbetas, label = txt, ymin = 0, ymax = dbetas)) +
      geom_linerange(colour = "blue") +
      geom_hline(yintercept = c(0, threshold, -threshold), colour = "red") +
      geom_point(colour = "blue", shape = 1) +
      geom_text(
        hjust = -0.2, nudge_x = 0.15, size = 4, family = "serif",
        fontface = "italic", colour = "darkred", na.rm = TRUE
      ) +
      annotate("text", x = Inf, y = y_text_pos, hjust = 1.1, vjust = 0,
               family = "serif", fontface = "italic", colour = "darkred", size = 4,
               label = paste("Threshold:", round(threshold, 2))) +
      xlab("Observation") +
      ylab("DFBETAS") +
      ggtitle(paste("Influence Diagnostics for", colnames(dfb)[i]))

    if (fixed_ylim) {
      p <- p + ylim(y_range)
    }

    myplots[[i]] <- p
    outliers[[i]] <- f
  }

  if (print_plot) {
    cowplot::plot_grid(plotlist = myplots, ncol = 2)
  } else {
    return(list(plots = myplots, outliers = outliers))
  }
}


## modiy cooks distance 
ols_plot_cooksd_chart_custom <- function(model, type = 1, threshold = NULL, print_plot = TRUE) {
  olsrr:::check_model(model)
  k <- olsrr:::ols_prep_cdplot_data(model, type)
  d <- olsrr:::ols_prep_outlier_obs(k)
  f <- olsrr:::ols_prep_cdplot_outliers(k)
  
  if (is.null(threshold)) {
    threshold <- k$ts
  }

  # Calculate upper limit and label position
  y_max <- max(d$cd, na.rm = TRUE)
  y_range <- range(d$cd, na.rm = TRUE)
  y_buffer <- 0.1 * diff(y_range)
  y_upper <- y_max + y_buffer
  y_text_pos <- y_upper - 0.01 * diff(c(y_range[1], y_upper))

  # Create plot
  p <- ggplot(d, aes(x = obs, y = cd, label = txt, ymin = min(cd), ymax = cd)) +
    geom_linerange(colour = "blue") +
    geom_point(shape = 1, colour = "blue") +
    geom_text(
      vjust = -1, size = 4, family = "serif", fontface = "italic",
      colour = "darkred", na.rm = TRUE
    ) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = threshold, colour = "red") +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dotted", colour = "red") +  # add 0.5 line
    annotate("text", x = Inf, y = y_text_pos, hjust = 1.1, vjust = 0,
             family = "serif", fontface = "italic", colour = "darkred", size = 4.5,
             label = paste("Threshold:", round(threshold, 3))) +
    ylim(0, y_upper) +
    xlab("Observation") +
    ylab("Cook's D") +
    ggtitle("Cook's Distance Plot")

  if (print_plot) {
    suppressWarnings(print(p))
  } else {
    return(list(plot = p, outliers = f, threshold = threshold))
  }
}


# modify DFFIT
ols_plot_dffits_custom <- function(model, size_adj_threshold = TRUE, print_plot = TRUE) {
  olsrr:::check_model(model)
  data <- olsrr:::ols_prep_dffits_plot_data(model, size_adj_threshold)

  d <- data$d
  threshold <- data$dft
  title <- data$title

  # Determine y-axis range with buffer
  y_max <- max(d$dbetas, na.rm = TRUE)
  y_min <- min(d$dbetas, na.rm = TRUE)
  buffer <- 0.1 * max(abs(c(y_max, y_min, threshold)))
  y_upper <- max(y_max, threshold) + buffer
  y_lower <- min(y_min, -threshold) - buffer

  # Position the threshold label just inside the top
  y_text_pos <- y_upper - 0.01 * (y_upper - y_lower)

  p <- ggplot(d, aes(x = obs, y = dbetas, label = txt, ymin = 0, ymax = data$dfm)) +
    geom_linerange(colour = "blue") +
    geom_hline(yintercept = c(0, threshold, -threshold), colour = "red") +
    geom_point(colour = "blue", shape = 1) +
    geom_text(
      hjust = -0.2, nudge_x = 0.15, size = 4.2,  # <- increased size
      family = "serif", fontface = "italic", colour = "darkred", na.rm = TRUE
    ) +
    annotate(
      "text", x = Inf, y = y_text_pos, hjust = 1.1, vjust = 0,
      family = "serif", fontface = "italic", colour = "darkred", size = 4.5,
      label = paste("Threshold:", round(threshold, 2))
    ) +
    ylim(y_lower, y_upper) +
    xlab("Observation") +
    ylab("DFFITS") +
    ggtitle(paste("DFFIT Plot"))

  if (print_plot) {
    suppressWarnings(print(p))
  } else {
    return(list(plot = p, outliers = data$f, threshold = round(threshold, 2)))
  }
}

plot_covratio <- function(model) {
  inf <- influence.measures(model)$infmat
  covr <- unname(inf[, "cov.r"])
  n    <- nobs(model)
  p    <- length(coef(model))  # includes intercept
  thr  <- 3 * p / n
  df   <- data.frame(obs = seq_along(covr), covr = covr)
  
  op <- par(mfrow = c(1,1))
  on.exit(par(op))
  plot(df$obs, df$covr, pch = 19, xlab = "Observation",
       ylab = "COVRATIO (cov.r)",
       main = "Influence on coefficient covariance (COVRATIO)")
  abline(h = 1, lty = 2)
  abline(h = 1 + thr, lty = 3)
  abline(h = 1 - thr, lty = 3)
  labs <- which(covr > 1 + thr | covr < 1 - thr)
  if (length(labs)) text(labs, covr[labs], labels = labs, pos = 3, cex = 0.8)
}



```

# Reference

Kalo Z, van den Akker LHM, Voko Z, Csana di M, Pitter JG (2019) Is there a fair allocation of healthcare research funds by the European Union? PLoS ONE 14(4): e0207046. <https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0207046>

# Disclosure

This reproducibility project was conducted to the best of our ability, with careful attention to statistical methods and assumptions. The research team comprises four senior biostatisticians (three of whom are accredited), with 20 to 30 years of experience in statistical modelling and analysis of healthcare data. While statistical assumptions play a crucial role in analysis, their evaluation is inherently subjective, and contextual knowledge can influence judgements about the importance of assumption violations. Differences in interpretation may arise among statisticians and researchers, leading to reasonable disagreements about methodological choices.

Our approach aimed to reproduce published analyses as faithfully as possible, using the details provided in the original papers. We acknowledge that other statisticians may have differing success in reproducing results due to variations in data handling and implicit methodological choices not fully described in publications. However, we maintain that research articles should contain sufficient detail for any qualified statistician to reproduce the analyses independently.

# Methods used in our reproducibility analyses

There were two parts to our study. First, 100 articles published in *PLOS ONE* were randomly selected from the health domain and sent for post-publication peer review by statisticians. Of these, 95 included linear regression analyses and were therefore assessed for reporting quality. The statisticians evaluated what was reported, including regression coefficients, 95% confidence intervals, and p-values, as well as whether model assumptions were described and how those assumptions were evaluated. This report provides a brief summary of the initial statistical review.

The second part of the study involved reproducing linear regression analyses for papers with available data to assess both computational and inferential reproducibility. All papers were initially assessed for data availability and the statistical software used. From those with accessible data, the first 20 papers (from the original random sample) were evaluated for computational reproducibility. Within each paper, individual linear regression models were identified and assigned a unique number. A maximum of three models per paper were selected for assessment. When more than three models were reported, priority was given to the final model or the primary models of interest as identified by the authors; any remaining models were selected at random.

To assess computational reproducibility, differences between the original and reproduced results were evaluated using absolute discrepancies and rounding error thresholds, tailored to the number of decimal places reported in each paper. Results for each reported statistic, e.g., regression coefficient, were categorised as *Reproduced*, *Incorrect Rounding*, or *Not Reproduced*, depending on how closely they matched the original values. Each paper was then classified as *Reproduced*, *Mostly Reproduced*, *Partially Reproduced*, or *Not Reproduced*. The mostly reproduced category included cases with minor rounding or typographical errors, whereas partially reproduced indicated substantial errors were observed, but some results were reproduced.

For models deemed at least partially computationally reproducible, inferential reproducibility was further assessed by examining whether statistical assumptions were met and by conducting sensitivity analyses, including bootstrapping where appropriate. We examined changes in standardized regression coefficients, which reflect the change in the outcome (in standard deviation units) for a one standard deviation increase in the predictor. Meaningful differences were defined as a relative change of 10% or more, or absolute differences of 0.1 (moderate) and 0.2 (substantial). When non-linear relationships were identified, inferential reproducibility was assessed by comparing model fit measures, including R², Akaike Information Criterion (AIC), and Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC). When the Gaussian distribution was not appropriate for the dependent variable, alternative distributions were considered, and model fit was evaluated using AIC and BIC.

Results from the reproduction of the Kalo et al. (2019) paper are presented below. An overall summary of results is presented first, followed by model-specific results organised within tab panels. Within each panel, the *Original results* tab displays the linear regression outputs extracted from the published paper. The *Reproduced results* tab presents estimates derived from the authors' shared data, along with a comprehensive assessment of linear regression assumptions. The *Differences* tab compares the original and reproduced models to assess computational reproducibility. Finally, the *Sensitivity analysis* tab evaluates inferential reproducibility by examining whether identified assumption violations meaningfully affected the results.

# Summary from statistical review

This paper investigated factors associated with grant allocation in the European Union. The outcome in the linear regression analyses was country-level grant funding. The Multivariable model appears consistent with a backward selection approach, although this was not explicitly described as such. Assumptions of linear regression were assessed using residual diagnostics and graphical checks. Collinearity and outliers were examined; however, influential observations were retained in the final models without accompanying sensitivity analyses.

# Data availability and software used

The authors provided the data in a Word (DOCX) file as supporting information, in a wide format. Importing the data was difficult and required additional cleaning due to the file format. The authors used Stata for the regression analyses.

# Regression sample

There were two multivariable models examining health research funding per 100,000 people, which where related to the main research question, with initial variable model which had predictors of (1) average gross domestic product per capita (GDP per capita), (2) average population size, (3) disease burden in disability-adjusted life years (DALY) per 100,000 inhabitants , and (4) excellence of research capabilities (citations). The second and final multivariable model, had DALY removed. There were also four univariate analysis, of which citations was selected randomly for reproduction.

# Computational reproducibility results

This paper was not computationally reproducible, as one of the three models could not be reproduced. The univariate analysis for citations was reproducible, and the final multivariable model was mostly reproducible, with only minor rounding differences. However, the initial multivariable model was not reproducible.

To investigate this further, the univariate association for DALYs was examined. The authors reported that “disease burden showed a statistically significant negative association of −97,410 EUR per 100,000 inhabitants, and per 1,000 additional DALYs per 100,000 inhabitants (p = 0.003; R² = 0.30).” While this presentation is confusing due to the simultaneous use of per 1,000 and per 100,000 scaling, rescaling the reproduced coefficient by 1,000 yields −97,727, matching the reported scale but not the exact value (paper: −97,410). This suggests that the discrepancy is not due solely to scaling; however, the reported p-value and R² were reproducible. In the initial multivariable model, the DALY coefficient was −30.15, whereas the reproduced estimate was −29.85. Although this difference was small and on the correct scale, all other coefficients and their associated statistics differed, suggesting that the data provided by the authors may differ from that used in the analyses.

# Inferential reproducibility results

This paper was not inferentially reproducible. The univariate model for citations was successfully reproduced, with standardized coefficients and confidence interval bounds below 0.10, and with consistent coefficient directions and statistical significance. However, the final multivariable model contained a highly influential observation (Cook’s distance = 3.9). Although the authors acknowledged this outlier, no sensitivity analysis was conducted to assess its impact. Sensitivity analyses conducted here indicated substantial changes in effect sizes and linearity conclusions (as described below).

To mitigate the influence of an extreme observation, funding and GDP per capita were log-transformed, while citations were retained on the original scale. Each additional citation was associated with an 8.2% increase in funding (95% CI: 3.7% to 12.9%, p \< 0.001). A 10% increase in GDP per capita was associated with an approximately 12% increase in funding. As the model was fitted on the log scale, fit statistics (e.g. R^2^, AIC, BIC) are not directly comparable with those from models on the original scale. The focus was therefore on obtaining a valid inferential model, assessed through residual diagnostics to ensure that the log transformation improved adherence to model assumptions and mitigated the impact of outliers. Although the influential observation continued to have some impact, its influence was substantially reduced, with Cook’s distance below 0.5. On the log scale, residual diagnostics did not indicate evidence of non-linearity in the association between GDP per capita and funding. Mild heteroscedasticity was observed, robust standard errors were used as a sensitivity analysis.

# Recommended Changes

-   `r File_format`
-   When influential outliers are identified, a sensitivity analysis is warranted. Appropriate approaches include refitting models with and without the influential observation.
-   There were nonlinear relationships observed in the data as well as large outliers, this can be appropriately modelled by log transforming data.
-   `r AdjR2`
-   `r datD`
-   `r Code`

```{r}

repdat <- data.frame(
  paper_ID = as.numeric(32),
  Title = as.character("Is there a fair allocation of healthcare research funds by the European Union?"),
  Summary = as.character("This paper investigated factors associated with grant allocation in the European Union. The outcome in the linear regression analyses was country-level grant funding. The Multivariable model appears consistent with a backward selection approach, although this was not explicitly described as such. Assumptions of linear regression were assessed using residual diagnostics and graphical checks. Collinearity and outliers were examined; however, influential observations were retained in the final models without accompanying sensitivity analyses."),
  Regression_sample =  as.character("There were two multivariable models examining whether health research funding per 100,000 people, which where related to the main research question, with initial variable model which had predictors of (1) average gross domestic product per capita (GDP per capita), (2) average population size, (3) disease burden in disability-adjusted life years (DALY) per 100,000 inhabitants , and (4) excellence of research capabilities (citations). The second and final multivariable model, had DALY removed. There were also four univariate analysis, of which citations was selected randomly for reproduction."),
  Data_comments = as.character("The authors provided the data in a Word (DOCX) file as supporting information, in a wide format. Importing the data was difficult and required additional cleaning due to the file format. The authors used Stata for the regression analyses."),
  Data_Availability_Statement = as.character("All relevant data are within the paper and its Supporting Information files."),
  DAS_correct = as.character("Yes"),
  Data_Notionally_Available = as.character("Yes"),
  Ethics_required_access_data = as.character("NA"),
  Permission_required_access_data = as.character("NA"),
  Data_Available = as.character("Yes"),
  Anyrawdata = as.character ("Yes"),
  HaveSupInfo = as.character ("Yes"),
  Data_location = as.character("Supporting Information"),
  Accessible_data_badge  = as.character("No"),
  Data_Reason_not_found  = as.character("NA"),
  Data_Dictionary = as.character("No"),
  Format_file = as.character("docx"),
  Format_data  = as.character("Wide"),
  Package = as.character("Stata"),
  Recommended_changes = as.character("Data underlying statistical analyses should be provided in a standard data format (e.g., CSV, XLSX, or similar) so it is machine readable. When influential outliers are identified, a sensitivity analysis is warranted. Appropriate approaches include refitting models with and without the influential observation.There were nonlinear relationship observed in the data as well as large outliers, this can be appropriately modelled by log transforming data. Adjusted R^2^ should be reported for multivariable models. Include a data dictionary. Consider creating a reproducible analysis workflow and sharing the code."),
  Reproduced = as.character("No"),
  Rep_comment = as.character("The data in docx format, made it more difficult to download. Some models reproduced and some models did not reproduce made it challanging."),
  Rating = as.numeric(2),
  Unclear_N = as.character("No"),
  Difficult_matching_variables = as.character ("No"),
  Rounding = as.character ("Yes"),
  Unclear_model = as.character ("No"),
  Typo = as.character ("No"),
  Data_Cleaning_required = as.character ("Yes"),
  Data_Reformatting_required = as.character ("No"),
  R1 = as.character("Reproduced"),
  R2 = as.character("Not reproduced"),
  R3 = as.character("Mostly reproduced"),
  E1 = as.character("Yes"),
  E2 = as.character("NA"),
  E3 = as.character("No"),
  E1_Dist = as.character("Gaussian"),
  E2_Dist  = as.character("NA"),
  E3_Dist  = as.character("Gaussian"),
  E1_Mixed = as.character("No"),
  E2_Mixed  = as.character("NA"),
  E3_Mixed = as.character("No"),
  E1_Linkfunction = as.character("Identity"),
  E2_Linkfunction = as.character("NA"),
  E3_Linkfunction  = as.character("Identity"),
  E1_RSE = as.character("Yes"),
  E2_RSE  = as.character("NA"),
  E3_RSE  = as.character("No"),
  E1_Nonlinear = as.character("No"),
  E2_Nonlinear  = as.character("NA"),
  E3_Nonlinear  = as.character("Yes"),
  E1_Transformation = as.character("No"),
  E2_Transformation  = as.character("NA"),
  E3_Transformation  = as.character("Yes"),
  E1_Regularisation = as.character("No"),
  E2_Regularisation = as.character("NA"),
  E3_Regularisation  = as.character("No"),
  E1_boot = as.character("Wild"),
  E2_boot  = as.character("NA"),
  E3_boot  = as.character("No"),
  Comp_reproduce = as.character("No"),
  Infer_reproduce = as.character("No"),
  Comp_comment = as.character("This paper was not computationally reproducible, as one of the three models could not be reproduced. The univariate analysis for citations was reproducible, and the final multivariable model was mostly reproducible, with only minor rounding differences. However, the initial multivariable model was not reproducible. To investigate this further, the univariate association for DALYs was examined. The authors reported that “disease burden showed a statistically significant negative association of −97,410 EUR per 100,000 inhabitants, and per 1,000 additional DALYs per 100,000 inhabitants (p = 0.003; R² = 0.30).” While this presentation is confusing due to the simultaneous use of per 1,000 and per 100,000 scaling, rescaling the reproduced coefficient by 1,000 yields −97,727, matching the reported scale but not the exact value (paper: −97,410). This suggests that the discrepancy is not due solely to scaling; however, the reported p-value and R² were reproducible. In the initial multivariable model, the DALY coefficient was −30.15, whereas the reproduced estimate was −29.85. Although this difference was small and on the correct scale, all other coefficients and their associated statistics differed, suggesting that the data provided by the authors may differ from that used in the analyses."),
  Infer_comment = as.character("This paper was not inferentially reproducible. The univariate model for citations was successfully reproduced, with standardized coefficients and confidence interval bounds below 0.10, and with consistent coefficient directions and statistical significance. However, the final multivariable model contained a highly influential observation (Cook’s distance = 3.9). Although the authors acknowledged this outlier, no sensitivity analysis was conducted to assess its impact. Sensitivity analyses conducted here indicated substantial changes in effect sizes and linearity conclusions.
To mitigate the influence of an extreme observation, funding and GDP per capita were log-transformed, while citations were retained on the original scale. Each additional citation was associated with an 8.2% increase in funding (95% CI: 3.7% to 12.9%, p < 0.001). A 10% increase in GDP per capita was associated with an approximately 12% increase in funding. As the model was fitted on the log scale, fit statistics (e.g. R2, AIC, BIC) are not directly comparable with those from models on the original scale. The focus was therefore on obtaining a valid inferential model, assessed through residual diagnostics to ensure that the log transformation improved adherence to model assumptions and mitigated the impact of outliers. Although the influential observation continued to have some impact, its influence was substantially reduced, with Cook’s distance below 0.5. On the log scale, residual diagnostics did not indicate evidence of non-linearity in the association between GDP per capita and funding. Mild heteroscedasticity was observed, robust standard errors were used as a sensitivity analysis."),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE)


write.csv(repdat, file="data/repdat_32.csv")


```

```{r}

# randomly selecting linear regressions
set.seed(1817253) 
random_numbers <- sample(1:4, 1)
# number chosen 2

```

```{r, results='hide'}


# Download the .docx file
url <- "https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0207046.s003"
download.file(url, destfile = "table_data.docx", mode = "wb")

# Read the DOCX file
doc <- read_docx("table_data.docx")

# List the tables in the document
tables <- docx_extract_all_tbls(doc)


data <- as.data.frame(tables[[1]]) %>% dplyr::rename(Average_GDP_PC= "Average.GDP.per.capita.between.2007.and.2016", Average_population ="Average.population.size.between.2007.and.2016",                                             DALY_per_100.000= DALY.per.100.000.inhabitants.in.2010, Citations = Citations.per.document.according.to.the.Scimago.medicine.index.between.1996.and.2016) %>% 
  mutate(across(c(Average_population, Average_GDP_PC, DALY_per_100.000, Citations), ~ as.numeric(str_remove_all(., ",")))) %>%
   mutate(Average_population = if_else(is.na(Average_population), 10492855, Average_population)) 




# Step 1: Download the image
url <- "https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article/figure/image?size=large&id=10.1371/journal.pone.0207046.t001"
image_path <- file.path(getwd(), "table_image.png")
GET(url, write_disk(image_path, overwrite = TRUE))

# Step 2: Extract text from the image using OCR
ocr_engine <- tesseract("eng")
text_from_image <- ocr(image_path, engine = ocr_engine)

# Display extracted text to inspect
cat(text_from_image)

# Step 3: Clean and structure the text data
# Split lines into vector format
text_lines <- unlist(strsplit(text_from_image, "\n"))

# Filter out lines with irrelevant information
text_lines <- text_lines[!grepl("inhabitants|EUR|doi|between", text_lines)]

# Combine split country names (e.g., "United Kingdom")
# Re-parse lines for better accuracy and manual tweaks
for (i in seq_along(text_lines)) {
  if (grepl("United$", text_lines[i])) {
    text_lines[i] <- paste(text_lines[i], text_lines[i + 1])
    text_lines <- text_lines[-(i + 1)]
  }
  if (grepl("Czech$", text_lines[i])) {
    text_lines[i] <- paste(text_lines[i], text_lines[i + 1])
    text_lines <- text_lines[-(i + 1)]
  }
}

# Extract structured data using regex
library(stringr)
data_list <- lapply(text_lines, function(line) {
  match <- str_match(line, "([A-Za-z ]+) ([0-9,]+) ([0-9,]+)")
  if (!is.na(match[1])) {
    country <- trimws(match[2])
    population <- as.numeric(gsub(",", "", match[3]))
    funding <- as.numeric(gsub(",", "", match[4]))
    return(c(Country = country, Population = population, Funding = funding))
  }
  return(NULL)
})

# Convert list to a data frame
df <- do.call(rbind, data_list) %>% as.data.frame(stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

# Convert Population and Funding columns to numeric
df$Population <- as.numeric(df$Population)
df$Funding <- as.numeric(df$Funding)

df<-df %>%
  mutate(Country = case_when(
    Country == "Czech" ~ "Czech Republic",
    Country == "United" ~ "United Kingdom",
    TRUE ~ Country  # keep original value if no match
  ))



dat<-merge(df, data, by="Country") %>% dplyr::rename(Funding_per_100000= Population,DALY_per_100000=DALY_per_100.000) %>% 
  mutate(population_quartile = ntile(Average_population, 4)) %>%  mutate(population_quartile = case_when(population_quartile == 1 ~ "1st quartile", population_quartile == 2 ~ "2nd quartile", population_quartile ==3 ~ "3rd quartile",
                                                                         population_quartile == 4 ~ "4th quartile")) %>% 
mutate(log_Funding_per_100000=log(Funding_per_100000)) %>% mutate(log_Average_GDP_PC=log(Average_GDP_PC))
  
# %>% filter(Country != "Luxembourg")






namey1<-"Health research funding per 100,000"
namey2<-"Health research funding per 100,000"
namey3<-"Health research funding per 100,000"


m1y <- "Funding_per_100000"
m1x1 <- "Citations"

m2y <- "Funding_per_100000"
m2x1 <- "Average_GDP_PC"
m2x2 <- "DALY_per_100000"
m2x3 <- "Citations"
m2x4 <- "population_quartile"

m3y <- "Funding_per_100000"
m3x1 <- "Average_GDP_PC"
m3x2 <- "Citations"
m3x3 <- "population_quartile"


predictors1 <- c("Citations")
con1 <- c("Citations")
factor_vars1 <- character(0)  # No factor variables
ref_levels1 <- character(0)  # No reference levels

predictors2 <- c("Average_GDP_PC", "DALY_per_100000", "Citations", "population_quartile")
con2 <- c("Average_GDP_PC", "DALY_per_100000", "Citations")
factor_vars2 <- c("population_quartile")  
ref_levels2 <- c("1st quartile") 


predictors3 <- c("Average_GDP_PC", "Citations", "population_quartile")
con3 <- c("Average_GDP_PC", "Citations")
factor_vars3 <- c("population_quartile")  
ref_levels3 <- c("1st quartile") 






# Construct the formula dynamically, need as many %s x variables
formula_str1 <- sprintf("%s ~ %s", m1y, m1x1)
model_formula1 <- as.formula(formula_str1)

formula_str2 <- sprintf("%s ~ %s + %s  + %s + %s", m2y, m2x1,  m2x2, m2x3, m2x4)
model_formula2 <- as.formula(formula_str2)

formula_str3 <- sprintf("%s ~ %s + %s + %s", m3y, m3x1,  m3x2 ,m3x3)
model_formula3 <- as.formula(formula_str3)

paper_ID<-32
num_models<-3

DALY<-lm(Funding_per_100000 ~ DALY_per_100000,data=dat)
DALY1 <- tbl_regression(  DALY,   estimate_fun = ~ style_number(.x, digits = 4)
) %>%   add_glance_table()


```

```{r, results='hide', fig.show='hide'}
# Automatically generate model names
model_names <- paste0("Model ", seq_len(num_models))

# Automatically gather model formulas
model_formulas <- mget(paste0("model_formula", seq_len(num_models)))

# Automatically gather predictors
predictor_sets <- mget(paste0("predictors", seq_len(num_models)))

# Automatically gather dependent variables
dependent_vars <- mget(paste0("m", seq_len(num_models), "y"))

# Automatically gather custom y-axis names
custom_y_names <- mget(paste0("namey", seq_len(num_models)))

# Automatically gather continuous variable lists
continuous_vars_list <- mget(paste0("con", seq_len(num_models)))

# Automatically gather factor variable lists
factor_vars_list <- mget(paste0("factor_vars", seq_len(num_models)))

# Automatically gather reference levels lists
ref_levels_list <- mget(paste0("ref_levels", seq_len(num_models)))


# Ensure that ref_levels_list has the same length as model_formulas
while (length(ref_levels_list) < length(model_formulas)) {
  ref_levels_list <- c(ref_levels_list, list(character(0)))  # Add empty lists to match the number of models
}

# Function to dynamically assign data frames to the global environment
assign_df <- function(name, df) {
  assign(name, df, envir = .GlobalEnv)
}


# Function to generate visreg plots
generate_visreg_plots <- function(model, predictors, dependent_var, custom_y_name, i) {
  plot_list <- list()
  model_coeffs <- names(coef(model))  # Get the names of the coefficients in the model
  
  # Dynamically calculate global y-limits for the entire model
  visreg_outputs <- lapply(predictors, function(predictor) visreg(model, predictor, plot = FALSE))
  y_limits <- range(unlist(lapply(visreg_outputs, function(x) c(x$res$visregRes, x$res$visregFit))), na.rm = TRUE)
  
  for (j in seq_along(predictors)) {
    predictor <- predictors[j]
    
    # Check if the predictor or its factor levels are in the model
    predictor_in_model <- any(grepl(predictor, model_coeffs))
    
    if (predictor_in_model) {
      visreg_output <- visreg(model, predictor, gg = TRUE, overlay = TRUE, points = list(size = 4, col = "black")) +
        theme_bw() +
        theme(
          axis.title = element_text(size = 24),  # Increase axis title size
          axis.text = element_text(size = 24),   # Increase axis text size
          plot.title = element_text(size = 16),  # Increase plot title size
          legend.text = element_text(size = 16)  # Increase legend text size
        ) +
        ylim(y_limits) +  # Apply global y-limits
        ylab(custom_y_name)  # Set the y-axis label to the custom name
      
      # Save each visreg plot individually
      ggsave(paste0("figures/visreg_plot_", i, "_", predictor, ".png"), 
             plot = visreg_output, width = 8, height = 8, dpi = 300)
      
      plot_list[[j]] <- visreg_output
    }
  }
  return(plot_list)
}

# Corrected loop to fit the model and generate plots
for (i in seq_along(model_formulas)) {
  model_formula <- model_formulas[[i]]
  model_name <- model_names[[i]]
  predictors <- predictor_sets[[i]]
  dependent_var <- dependent_vars[[i]]
  custom_y_name <- custom_y_names[[i]]
  continuous_vars <- continuous_vars_list[[i]]
  factor_vars <- factor_vars_list[[i]]
  
  # Check if ref_levels_list has an entry for the current model
  if (i > length(ref_levels_list)) {
    cat("Warning: Index", i, "is out of bounds for ref_levels_list, which has length", length(ref_levels_list), "\n")
    next  # Skip to the next iteration if the index is out of bounds
  }
  
  ref_levels <- ref_levels_list[[i]]
  
  # Ensure that ref_levels are single values
  validated_ref_levels <- list()
  for (j in seq_along(factor_vars)) {
    if (length(ref_levels[j]) == 1) {
      validated_ref_levels[[j]] <- list(var = factor_vars[j], ref = ref_levels[j])
    } else {
      warning(paste("Skipping reference level for", factor_vars[j], "as it is not a single value."))
    }
  }
  
  # Fit the linear model
  model <- lm(model_formula, data = dat)
  
  
  
  # Create the arguments list dynamically for linReg
  linReg_args <- list(
    data = dat,
    dep = dependent_var,
    covs = continuous_vars,  # Use only continuous predictors here
    blocks = list(predictors),  # Include both continuous and categorical predictors in blocks
    r2Adj = TRUE,
    aic = TRUE,
    rmse = TRUE,
    modelTest = TRUE,
    anova = TRUE,
    ci = TRUE,
    stdEst = TRUE,
    ciStdEst = TRUE,
    norm = TRUE,
    qqPlot = TRUE,
    resPlots = TRUE,
    durbin = TRUE,
    collin = TRUE,
    cooks = TRUE
  )
  
 # Add factors and reference levels if present
if (length(factor_vars) > 0) {
  linReg_args$factors <- factor_vars
  if (length(validated_ref_levels) > 0) {
    linReg_args$refLevels <- validated_ref_levels
  }
}

# 1. Dummy-coded coefficients (reference group coding)
linReg_args_dummy <- linReg_args
linReg_args_dummy$intercept <- "refLevel"  # correct argument
M <- do.call(jmv::linReg, linReg_args_dummy)

# 2. ANOVA table relative to grand mean (effect coding)
linReg_args_anova <- linReg_args
linReg_args_anova$intercept <- "grandMean"
M_anova <- do.call(jmv::linReg, linReg_args_anova)

  
  # Process and store coefficients
  coefficients_df <- as.data.frame(M$models[[1]]$coef) %>%  
    mutate(Model = i, paper_ID = paper_ID, origin = "Reproduced") %>%
    mutate(n = 1:nrow(M$models[[1]]$coef$asDF))
  
  assign_df(paste0("R_coefficients_raw_df_", i), coefficients_df) 
  
  assign_df(paste0("R_coefficients_flextable_", i), coefficients_df %>%
              dplyr::select(term, est, se, lower, upper, t, p) %>% 
              mutate(p = sapply(p, round_p)) %>%
              flextable() %>%
              set_table_properties(layout = "autofit") %>%
              set_header_labels(term = "Term", est = "B", se = "SE", lower = "Lower", upper = "Upper", p = "p-value") %>%
              colformat_double(digits = dig) %>%
              add_footer_lines("SE = Standard error; Lower = lower confidence interval; Upper = upper confidence interval."))
  
  # Replicate rows for fit statistics
  fit <- M$modelFit$asDF %>% 
    mutate(Model = i, paper_ID = paper_ID, origin = "Reproduced") %>% 
    cbind(dplyr::select(coefficients_df, term, n)) %>% select(-model)
  assign_df(paste0("R_fit_stats_raw_df_", i), fit)
  
  assign_df(paste0("R_fit_stats_flextable_", i), M$modelFit$asDF %>%
              select(-model) %>%  
              mutate(p = sapply(p, round_p)) %>%
              mutate(across(c(df1, df2), ~ Sround(.x, 0))) %>%
              flextable() %>%
              set_header_labels(r = "R", r2 = "R2", r2Adj = "R2Adj", aic = "AIC", rmse = "RMSE", f = "F", df1 = "DF1", df2 = "DF2", p = "p-value") %>%
              set_table_properties(layout = "autofit") %>%
              compose(i = 1, j = "r2", part = "header", value = as_paragraph("R", as_sup("2"))) %>%
              compose(i = 1, j = "r2Adj", part = "header", value = as_paragraph("R", as_sup("2"), "Adj")) %>%
              colformat_double(digits = 3) %>%
              add_footer_lines(as_paragraph(
                as_i("R"), as_sup("2"), " Adj = Adjusted ", as_i("R"), as_sup("2"), "; ",
                "AIC = Akaike Information Criterion; RMSE = The Root Mean Squared Error; DF1 = Degrees of freedom for the model; DF2 = Degrees of freedom for the residuals."
              )))
  
  # Process and store ANOVA table
  assign_df(paste0("R_anova_raw_df_", i), M_anova$models[[1]]$anova$asDF %>%  
              mutate(Model = i, paper_ID = paper_ID, origin = "Reproduced", n = c(2:nrow(.), 1)))
  
  assign_df(paste0("R_anova_flextable_", i), M_anova$models[[1]]$anova$asDF %>%
              mutate(across(c(df), ~ Sround(.x, 0))) %>%
              mutate(p = sapply(p, round_p)) %>%
              mutate(F = ifelse(F == "NA", "", F)) %>%
              flextable() %>%
              set_table_properties(layout = "autofit") %>%
              set_header_labels(term = "Term", ss = "SS", df = "DF", ms = "MS", p = "p-value") %>%
              colformat_double(digits = dig) %>%
              add_footer_lines("SS = Sum of Squares; DF = Degrees of freedom; MS = Mean Square; Calculated using type III SS."))
  
  
  height_per_row <- 400  
  
  # Calculate the height based on the number of rows
  num_rows <- nrow(M$models[[1]]$anova$asDF)
  calculated_height <- num_rows * height_per_row
  
  # Ensure a minimum height in case there are very few rows
  min_height <- 1200  # Set a minimum height
  final_height <- max(calculated_height, min_height)
  
  height_per_row1 <- 3  
  
  # Calculate the height based on the number of rows
  num_rows1 <- nrow(M$models[[1]]$anova$asDF)
  calculated_height1 <- num_rows1 * height_per_row1
  
  # Ensure a minimum height in case there are very few rows
  min_height1 <- 12  # Set a minimum height
  final_height1 <- max(calculated_height1, min_height1)
  
  
  
  # Process and store normality tests (KS and SW separately)
  assign_df(paste0("R_normality_ks_raw_df_", i), tidy(ks.test(augment(model)$.std.resid, "pnorm")) %>%
              mutate(Model = i, paper_ID = paper_ID, origin = "Reproduced"))
  
  assign_df(paste0("R_normality_ks_flextable_", i), tidy(ks.test(augment(model)$.std.resid, "pnorm")) %>%
              mutate(p.value = sapply(p.value, round_p)) %>% 
              dplyr::select(-alternative) %>% 
              flextable() %>%
              set_table_properties(layout = "autofit") %>%
              set_header_labels(statistic = "Statistic", p.value = "p-value", method = "Method") %>%
              colformat_double(digits = dig))
  
  assign_df(paste0("R_normality_sw_raw_df_", i), tidy(shapiro.test(augment(model)$.std.resid)) %>%
              mutate(Model = i, paper_ID = paper_ID, origin = "Reproduced"))
  
  assign_df(paste0("R_normality_sw_flextable_", i), tidy(shapiro.test(augment(model)$.std.resid)) %>%
              mutate(p.value = sapply(p.value, round_p)) %>% 
              flextable() %>%
              set_table_properties(layout = "autofit") %>%
              set_header_labels(statistic = "Statistic", p.value = "p-value", method = "Method") %>%
              colformat_double(digits = dig))
  
  # Process and store homoscedasticity test
  assign_df(paste0("R_homoscedasticity_raw_df_", i), tidy(bptest(model)) %>%
              mutate(Model = i, paper_ID = paper_ID, origin = "Reproduced"))
  
  assign_df(paste0("R_homoscedasticity_flextable_", i), tidy(bptest(model)) %>%
              mutate(p.value = sapply(p.value, round_p)) %>%
              mutate(across(c(parameter), ~ Sround(.x, 0))) %>% 
              flextable() %>%
              set_table_properties(layout = "autofit") %>%
              set_header_labels(statistic = "Statistic", p.value = "p-value", method = "Method", parameter = "Parameter") %>%
              colformat_double(digits = dig))
  
  
   # Compute influence measures and store in a dataframe
influence_measures <- data.frame(influence.measures(model)$infmat) %>%
  rownames_to_column("ID") %>%
  dplyr::select(-cook.d, -hat) %>% 
  mutate(across(-ID, ~ round(.x, 3)))


# Assign dataframe dynamically
assign_df(paste0("R_influence_measures_raw_df_", i), influence_measures)

# Compute descriptive statistics for influence measures
influence_summary <- as.data.frame(describe(influence_measures[-1])) %>%   rownames_to_column("term") %>% 
  select(term, n, mean, sd, median, min, max, skew, kurtosis) 


# Cook's Distance plot
cooks_threshold <- ols_plot_cooksd_chart_custom(model, print_plot = TRUE)
ggsave(
  filename = paste0("figures/cooks_threshold_", i, ".png"),
  plot = cooks_threshold,
  width = 8, height = 6, dpi = 300
)

# DFFITS plot
dffit_threshold <- ols_plot_dffits_custom(model, print_plot = TRUE)
ggsave(
  filename = paste0("figures/dffit_threshold_", i, ".png"),
  plot = dffit_threshold,
  width = 8, height = 6, dpi = 300
)


# Get the combined plot
dbetas_threshold <- ols_plot_dfbetas_fixed_y(model, print_plot = TRUE, fixed_ylim = TRUE)
  ggsave(
    filename = paste0("figures/dbetas_threshold_", i, ".png"),
    plot = dbetas_threshold,
    width = 10, height = 10, dpi = 300
  )
  
  # Assign the object with a dynamic name
  assign(paste0("dbetas_threshold_", i), dbetas_threshold)

 
 
  
   # Process and store description of residuals
  assign_df(paste0("R_description_flextable_", i), as.data.frame(describe(augment(model))) %>% 
              rownames_to_column("term") %>%
              mutate(term = if_else(term == ".hat", ".leverage", term)) %>%
              filter(term != ".rownames*") %>% 
              mutate(term = ifelse(row_number() == 1, custom_y_name, term)) %>%  # Replace the first value of 'term'
              mutate(across(c(n), ~ Sround(.x, 0))) %>%
              dplyr::select(term, n, mean, sd, median, min, max, skew, kurtosis) %>% 
              rbind(influence_summary) %>% 
              flextable() %>%
              set_table_properties(layout = "autofit") %>%
              set_header_labels(term = "Term", n = "N", mean = "Mean", sd = "SD", median = "Median", min = "Min", 
                                max = "Max", skew = "Skewness", kurtosis = "Kurtosis") %>%
              add_footer_lines("* categorical variable") %>%
              colformat_double(digits = dig))
  
  # Process and independence test
  assign_df(paste0("R_independence_raw_df_", i), M$models[[1]]$assump$durbin$asDF %>% 
              mutate(Model = i, paper_ID = paper_ID, origin = "Reproduced"))
  
  # Process and store independence test
  assign_df(paste0("R_independence_flextable_", i), M$models[[1]]$assump$durbin$asDF %>%
              rename("Statistic" = "dw", "AutoCorrelation" = "autoCor") %>%
              mutate(p = sapply(p, round_p)) %>%
              flextable() %>%
              set_table_properties(layout = "autofit") %>%
              set_header_labels(p = "p-value") %>%
              colformat_double(digits = dig))
  
  # Scatter plot with smoothers for each predictor
  scatter_plots <- lapply(predictors, function(predictor) {
    ggplot(dat, aes_string(x = predictor, y = dependent_var)) +
      geom_point(color = "black", size = 2.5) +  # Adjust the size as needed
      geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "blue", se = FALSE) +
      geom_smooth(method = "gam", color = "red", se = FALSE) +
      labs(x = predictor, y = custom_y_name) +
      theme_bw()+
      theme(
        axis.title = element_text(size = 18),  # Adjust the size as needed
        axis.text = element_text(size = 18)    # Adjust the size as needed
      )
  })
  
  # Arrange the scatter plots in a grid
  grid_plot <- do.call("grid.arrange", c(scatter_plots, ncol = 2))
  
  # Save the grid plot to a file
  ggsave(paste0("figures/scatter_plot_", i, ".png"), plot = grid_plot, width = 16, height = final_height1)
  dev.off() # Close the graphical device to prevent plotting in the output
  
  

  
 
  # Save Cook's distance plot
  cooks_plot <- influencePlot(model, xlab = "Leverage", ylab = "Studentized Residuals") %>%
    rownames_to_column("ID") %>%
    rename("Leverage" = "Hat") %>%
    arrange(CookD) %>% 
    left_join(influence_measures, by = "ID") %>%
    flextable() %>%
    add_footer_lines("StudRes = studentized residuals; CookD = Cook's Distance a combined measure of leverage and influence. DFBETAS (dfb.*) measures how much a specific regression coefficient changes (in standard errors) when an observation is removed; DFFITS measures how much the fitted (predicted) value for an observation changes (in standard deviations) when that observation is removed; cov.r = coefficient covariance ratio which measures how much the overall variance (precision) of the coefficients changes when that observation is removed.") %>%
      colformat_double(digits = 3)
  
  # Create flextable distance plot
  assign_df(paste0("R_cooks_distance_flextable_", i), cooks_plot)
  
  # Save the Cook's distance plot to a PNG file
  png(filename = paste0("figures/cooks_plots_", i, ".png"), width = 1600, height = 1600, res = 300)
  influencePlot(model, xlab = "Leverage", ylab = "Studentized Residuals")
  dev.off() # Close the graphical device to prevent plotting in the output
  
  # Process and store collinearity diagnostics
  assign_df(paste0("R_collinearity_raw_df_", i), M$models[[1]]$assump$collin$asDF %>% 
              add_row(.before = 1) %>% mutate(Model = i, paper_ID = paper_ID, origin = "Reproduced") %>%
              slice(2:nrow(M$models[[1]]$anova$asDF)))
  
  # Create flextable collinearity diagnostics
  assign_df(paste0("R_collinearity_flextable_", i), M$models[[1]]$assump$collin$asDF %>%
              flextable() %>%
              set_table_properties(layout = "autofit") %>%
              set_header_labels(vif = "VIF", tol = "Tolerance", term = "Term") %>%
              add_footer_lines("VIF = Variance Inflation Factor.") %>%
              colformat_double(digits = dig))
  
 # Save QQ plot using the car package
png(filename = paste0("figures/qq_plot_", i, ".png"), width = 800, height = 800, res = 100)

# Generate the QQ plot using car::qqPlot
qqPlot(
  model,
  distribution = "norm",  # Use "norm" for normality
  id = FALSE,             # Turn off labeling of extreme points
  main = "QQ Plot with 95% CI (Normal Distribution)"
)

dev.off()

# Save COVratio plot 
png(filename = paste0("figures/covratio_plot_", i, ".png"), width = 800, height = 800, res = 100)
plot_covratio(model)

dev.off()
  
  
 # Save the component-plus-residual plots to a PNG file with higher resolution and adjusted dimensions
  png(filename = paste0("figures/res_plot_", i, ".png"), width = 2000, height = final_height, res = 300)
  resplot <- car::residualPlots(model, layout = c(ceiling(nrow(M$models[[1]]$anova$asDF)/2), 2), type = "rstandard", ylab = "standardized residuals", col = "black", pch = 16, cex = 0.6, ylim = range(pmin(-3, rstandard(model)), pmax(3, rstandard(model)))) 
  dev.off()
  
  # Initialize ResPlot
  ResPlot <- as.data.frame(resplot)
  
  # Check if ResPlot has the expected columns and more than one row
  if(nrow(ResPlot) > 1 && all(c("Pr(>|Test stat|)", "Test stat") %in% names(ResPlot))) {
    ResPlot <- ResPlot %>% 
      mutate(n = c(2:nrow(.), 1), Model = i, paper_ID = paper_ID, origin = "Reproduced") %>% 
      rename("p.value" = "Pr(>|Test stat|)", "Statistic" = "Test stat") %>% 
      rownames_to_column("term") %>% 
      mutate(Results = ifelse(p.value < 0.05, "Linearity violation", "No linearity violation"))
  } else {
    # Handle the case where no proper lack-of-fit test is available
    ResPlot <- data.frame(term = "No possible lack-of-fit tests",
                          n = NA, Model = i, paper_ID = paper_ID, 
                          origin = "Reproduced", p.value = NA, 
                          Statistic = NA, Results = NA)
  }
  
  # Assign the residuals data frame to a dynamically named object
  assign_df(paste0("R_residuals_raw_df_", i), ResPlot)
  
  # Generate the flextable based on the ResPlot data
  ResPlotflex <- ResPlot %>% 
    select(-Model, -n, -paper_ID, -origin) %>% 
    mutate(p.value = sapply(p.value, round_p)) %>%
    flextable() %>% 
    set_header_labels(term = "Term", p.value = "p-value") %>%
    autofit() %>% 
    add_footer_lines("Specification test for predictors using quadratic tests, for fitted values curvature is tested through Tukey's one-degree-of-freedom test for nonadditivity.") %>%
      colformat_double(digits = 3)
  
  # Assign the flextable to a dynamically named object
  assign_df(paste0("R_residuals_flextable_", i), ResPlotflex)
  
  # Generate visreg plots for the current model
  visreg_plots <- generate_visreg_plots(model, predictors, dependent_var, custom_y_name, i)
  
  if (length(visreg_plots) > 0) {
    # Arrange the plots in a grid if there are any valid plots
    reg_plot <- do.call("grid.arrange", c(visreg_plots, ncol = 2))
    
    # Save the grid plot to a file
    ggsave(paste0("figures/reg_plot_", i, ".png"), plot = reg_plot, width = 20, height = final_height1)
  } else {
    message("No valid visreg plots generated for model ", model_name)
  }
  
  
}


```

```{r}

# Function to dynamically assign data frames to the global environment
assign_df <- function(name, df) {
  assign(name, df, envir = .GlobalEnv)
}

# Define the predictors globally
term1 <- R_coefficients_raw_df_1$term
term2 <- R_coefficients_raw_df_2$term
term3 <- R_coefficients_raw_df_3$term



# List of predictors for each model
predictor_sets <- list(predictors1, predictors2, predictors3 )
term_sets <- list(term1,term2,term3)

# List of coefficients, fit statistics, and ANOVA data for original models
original_coefs <- list(
  data.frame(term = c(term1),
             est = c(NA, "139046"),
             se = c(NA, NA),
             lower = c(NA,NA),
             upper = c(NA, NA),
             t = c(NA, NA), 
             p = c(NA, "<0.001"),
             stdEst = c(NA, NA), 
             stdEstLower = c(NA, NA), 
             stdEstUpper = c(NA, NA)), 
            
   data.frame(term = c(term2),
             est = c(NA, "18.41", "-30.15", "100283", NA, "673962", "633305", "190753"),
             se = c(NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA),
             lower = c(NA, "-3.64", "-79.93", "60269", NA, "69270", "6829", "-436460"),
             upper = c(NA, "40.46", "19.64", "140298", NA, "1278655", "1259782", "1352454"),
             t = c(NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA),
             p = c(NA, "0.097", "0.222", "<0.001", NA, "0.031", "0.048", "0.534"),
             stdEst = c(NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA), 
             stdEstLower = c(NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA), 
             stdEstUpper = c(NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA)), 
             
  data.frame(term = c(term3),
             est = c(NA, "27.55", "97560", NA, "700203", "721330", "293956"),
             se = c(NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA),
             lower = c(NA, "11.32", "57385", NA, "90777", "105599", "-315950"),
             upper = c(NA, "43.79", "137736", NA, "1309630", "1337061", "903861"),
             t = c(NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA),
             p = c(NA, "0.002", "<0.001", NA, "0.026", "0.024", "0.328"),
             stdEst = c(NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA), 
             stdEstLower = c(NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA), 
             stdEstUpper = c(NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA)))

original_fit_stats <- list(
  data.frame(r = NA, r2 = "0.64", r2Adj = NA, aic = NA, rmse = NA, f = NA, df1 = NA, df2 = NA, p = NA),
  data.frame(r = NA, r2 = "0.83", r2Adj = NA, aic = NA, rmse = NA, f = NA, df1 = NA, df2 = NA, p = NA),
  data.frame(r = NA, r2 = "0.82", r2Adj = NA, aic = NA, rmse = NA, f = NA, df1 = NA, df2 = NA, p = NA)
)

original_anova <- list(
  data.frame(term = c(predictors1, "Residuals"),
             ss = c(NA, NA),
             df = c(NA, NA),
             ms = c(NA, NA),
             F = c(NA, NA),
             p = c(NA, NA)),
  data.frame(term = c(predictors2, "Residuals"),
             ss = c(NA, NA, NA, NA, NA),
             df = c(NA, NA, NA, NA, NA),
             ms = c(NA, NA, NA, NA, NA),
             F = c(NA, NA, NA, NA, NA),
             p = c(NA, NA, NA, "0.082", NA)),
  data.frame(term = c(predictors3, "Residuals"),
             ss = c(NA, NA, NA, NA),
             df = c(NA, NA, NA, NA),
             ms = c(NA, NA, NA, NA),
             F = c(NA, NA, NA, NA),
             p = c(NA, NA,"0.067", NA))
)

# Function to format coefficients data
format_coefs <- function(coefs_df, model_num, type) {
  coefs_df <- coefs_df %>% mutate(Model = model_num, paper_ID = paper_ID, origin = type, n = 1:nrow(coefs_df))
  coefs_df %>%
    dplyr::select(term, est, se, lower, upper, t, p) %>%  
     flextable() %>%
    set_table_properties(layout = "autofit") %>%
    set_header_labels(term = "Term", lower = "Lower", upper = "Upper", p = "p-value", est = "B", se = "SE", t = "t") %>%
    add_footer_lines("SE = Standard error; Lower = lower confidence interval; Upper = upper confidence interval.")
}

# Function to format fit statistics data
format_fit_stats <- function(fit_stats_df, coef_df, model_num, type) {
  terms <- coef_df %>% select(term, n) %>% distinct()
  fit_stats_df <- fit_stats_df %>%
    slice(rep(1, nrow(terms))) %>%
    mutate(Model = model_num, paper_ID = paper_ID, origin = type) %>%
    cbind(terms)
  fit_stats_df %>%
    slice(1) %>% 
    dplyr::select(r, r2, r2Adj, aic, rmse, f, df1, df2, p) %>%
    mutate(across(c(df1, df2), ~ Sround(.x, 0))) %>%
    mutate(df1 = ifelse(df1=="NA", "", df1)) %>% 
    mutate(df2 = ifelse(df2=="NA", "", df2)) %>% 
    flextable() %>%
    set_table_properties(layout = "autofit") %>%
    set_header_labels(r = "R", r2 = "R2", r2Adj = "R2Adj", aic = "AIC", rmse = "RMSE", f = "F", p = "p-value", df1 = "DF1", df2 = "DF2") %>%
    compose(i = 1, j = "r2", part = "header", value = as_paragraph("R", as_sup("2"))) %>%
    compose(i = 1, j = "r2Adj", part = "header", value = as_paragraph("R", as_sup("2"), "Adj")) %>%
   add_footer_lines(as_paragraph(
    as_i("R"), as_sup("2"), " Adj = Adjusted ", as_i("R"), as_sup("2"), "; ",
    "AIC = Akaike Information Criterion; RMSE = The Root Mean Squared Error; DF1 = Degrees of freedom for the model; DF2 = Degrees of freedom for the residuals."
))
}

# Function to format ANOVA data
format_anova <- function(anova_df, coef_df, model_num, type) {
  anova_df <- anova_df %>% mutate(Model = model_num, paper_ID = paper_ID, origin = type, n = c(2:nrow(.), 1))
  anova_df %>%
    dplyr::select(-Model, -paper_ID, -origin, -n) %>%
   # mutate(across(c(df), ~ Sround(.x, 0))) %>% 
    mutate(df = ifelse(is.na(df), "", df)) %>%
    flextable() %>%
    set_table_properties(layout = "autofit") %>%
    set_header_labels(term = "Term", ms = "MS", ss = "SS", p = "p-value", df = "DF") %>%
    add_footer_lines("SS = Sum of Squares; DF = Degrees of freedom; MS = Mean Square.")
}

# Loop to handle original data
for (i in seq_along(original_coefs)) {
  # Format coefficients
  O_coefficients_raw_df <- original_coefs[[i]] %>% mutate(Model = i, paper_ID = paper_ID, origin = "Original", n = 1:nrow(.))
  assign_df(paste0("O_coefficients_raw_df_", i), O_coefficients_raw_df)
  
  O_coefficients_flextable <- format_coefs(original_coefs[[i]], i, "Original")
  assign_df(paste0("O_coefficients_flextable_", i), O_coefficients_flextable)
  
  # Format fit statistics
  O_fit_stats_raw_df <- original_fit_stats[[i]] %>%
    slice(rep(1, nrow(O_coefficients_raw_df))) %>%
    mutate(Model = i, paper_ID = paper_ID, origin = "Original") %>%
    cbind(select(O_coefficients_raw_df, term, n))
  assign_df(paste0("O_fit_stats_raw_df_", i), O_fit_stats_raw_df)
  
  O_fit_stats_flextable <- format_fit_stats(original_fit_stats[[i]], O_coefficients_raw_df, i, "Original")
  assign_df(paste0("O_fit_stats_flextable_", i), O_fit_stats_flextable)
  
  # Format ANOVA table
  O_anova_raw_df <- original_anova[[i]] %>% mutate(Model = i, paper_ID = paper_ID, origin = "Original", n = c(2:nrow(.), 1))
  assign_df(paste0("O_anova_raw_df_", i), O_anova_raw_df)
  
  O_anova_flextable <- format_anova(original_anova[[i]], O_coefficients_raw_df, i, "Original")
  assign_df(paste0("O_anova_flextable_", i), O_anova_flextable)
}




```

```{r}
# Function to dynamically assign data frames to the global environment
assign_df <- function(name, df) {
  assign(name, df, envir = .GlobalEnv)
}

# Function to bind rows, rename columns, and select specific columns
bind_and_rename <- function(models_list, prefix, exclude_cols) {
  df <- bind_rows(models_list)
  
  # Ensure exclude columns exist before renaming
  existing_cols <- intersect(colnames(df), exclude_cols)
  
  df <- df %>%
    rename_with(~ paste0(prefix, .), -all_of(existing_cols)) %>%
    dplyr::select(-matches(paste0(prefix, "origin")))
  
  return(df)
}

# Automatically gather original and reproduced coefficients data frames
origCoef_dfs <- mget(paste0("O_coefficients_raw_df_", seq_len(num_models)))
repCoef_dfs <- mget(paste0("R_coefficients_raw_df_", seq_len(num_models)))

# Bind and rename coefficients
origCoef <- bind_and_rename(origCoef_dfs, "O_", c("Model", "paper_ID", "n", "term"))
repCoef <- bind_and_rename(repCoef_dfs, "R_", c("Model", "paper_ID", "n", "term"))

# Automatically gather original and reproduced fit statistics data frames
origfit_dfs <- mget(paste0("O_fit_stats_raw_df_", seq_len(num_models)))
repfit_dfs <- mget(paste0("R_fit_stats_raw_df_", seq_len(num_models)))

# Bind and rename fit statistics
origfit <- bind_and_rename(origfit_dfs, "O_", c("Model", "paper_ID", "n", "term"))
repfit <- bind_and_rename(repfit_dfs, "R_", c("Model", "paper_ID", "n", "term"))

# Merge coefficients
df_list <- list(origCoef, repCoef)
C <- df_list %>% reduce(full_join, by = c("Model", "paper_ID", "n", "term"))

# Calculate changes and categorize them
C <- C %>%
   mutate(
    O_estN = as.numeric(O_est),
     O_tN = as.numeric(O_t),
    O_lowerN = as.numeric(O_lower),
    O_upperN = as.numeric(O_upper),
     O_seN=  as.numeric( O_se),
     R_seN = R_se, 
    R_se = as.character(R_se),
    O_pN = as.numeric(O_p),
    O_pN = ifelse(O_p == "<0.001", 0.00099, as.numeric(O_p)),
    R_estN =  R_est,
    R_tN =  R_t,
    R_lowerN = R_lower,
    R_upperN = R_upper,
    R_pN = R_p,
    R_est = as.character(R_est),
    R_t = as.character(R_t),
    R_lower = as.character(R_lower),
    R_upper = as.character( R_upper),
    R_p = as.character(R_p),
        changeB = R_estN - O_estN,
    change.t = R_tN - O_tN,
    change.lci = R_lowerN - O_lowerN,
    change.uci = R_upperN - O_upperN,
    change.se = R_seN -O_seN, 
    R_pN = if_else(R_pN < 0.001, 0.00099, R_pN),
    changep = R_pN - O_pN,
        SigChange = case_when(
      O_pN < 0.05 & R_pN < 0.05 ~ "Remains significant",
      O_pN >= 0.05 & R_pN >= 0.05 ~ "Remains non-sig",
      O_pN < 0.05 & R_pN >= 0.05 ~ "Change from sig to non-sig",
      O_pN >= 0.05 & R_pN < 0.05 ~ "Change from non-sig to sig"),
    SigChangeDirection = case_when(
      O_pN < 0.05 & R_pN < 0.05 & (sign(O_estN) != sign(R_estN)) ~ "Remains sig, B changes direction",
      O_pN < 0.05 & R_pN < 0.05 & (sign(O_estN) == sign(R_estN)) ~ "Remains sig, B same direction",
      O_pN >= 0.05 & R_pN < 0.05 & (sign(O_estN) == sign(R_estN) | sign(O_estN) != sign(R_estN)) ~ "Non-sig to sig, B same direction",
      O_pN < 0.05 & R_pN >= 0.05 & (sign(O_estN) != sign(R_estN) | sign(O_estN) == sign(R_estN)) ~ "Sig to non-sig, B changes direction",
      O_pN >= 0.05 & R_pN >= 0.05 & (sign(O_estN) != sign(R_estN)) ~ "Remains non-sig, B changes direction",
      O_pN >= 0.05 & R_pN >= 0.05 & (sign(O_estN) == sign(R_estN)) ~ "Remains non-sig, B same direction",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_))


# Merge fit statistics
df_list2 <- list(origfit, repfit)
F <- df_list2 %>% reduce(full_join, by = c("Model", "paper_ID", "n", "term"))

# Calculate changes and categorize them
F <- F %>%
    mutate(
    O_r2N = as.numeric(O_r2),
     O_r2AdjN = as.numeric(O_r2Adj),
     O_fN = as.numeric(O_f),
    O_pN = as.numeric(O_p),
    O_pN = ifelse(O_p == "<0.001", 0.00099, as.numeric(O_p)),
    R_r2N =R_r2,
    R_r2AdjN =R_r2Adj,
    R_fN = R_f,
    R_pN = R_p,
    R_r2 = as.character(R_r2),
    R_r2Adj = as.character(R_r2Adj),
    R_f = as.character(R_f),
    R_p = as.character(R_p),
    changeR2 = R_r2N - O_r2N,
    changeR2Adj = R_r2AdjN - O_r2AdjN,
    Change.f = R_fN - O_fN,
    Change.df1 = R_df1 - O_df1,
    Change.df2 = R_df2 - O_df2,
    Reproduce.df1 = case_when(
      Change.df1 == 0 ~ "Reproduced",
      Change.df1 < 0 | Change.df1 > 0 ~ "Not Reproduced",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_),
    Reproduce.df2 = case_when(
      Change.df2 == 0 ~ "Reproduced",
      Change.df2 < 0 | Change.df2 > 0 ~ "Not Reproduced",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_))


# Automatically gather original and reproduced ANOVA data frames
origANOVA_dfs <- mget(paste0("O_anova_raw_df_", seq_len(num_models)))
repANOVA_dfs <- mget(paste0("R_anova_raw_df_", seq_len(num_models)))

# Bind and rename ANOVA statistics
origANOVA <- bind_and_rename(origANOVA_dfs, "O_", c("Model", "paper_ID", "n", "term")) %>%
  mutate(term = ifelse(term == "Residuals", "Intercept", term))

repANOVA <- bind_and_rename(repANOVA_dfs, "R_", c("Model", "paper_ID", "n", "term")) %>%
  mutate(term = ifelse(term == "Residuals", "Intercept", term))

# Merge ANOVA statistics
df_list3 <- list(origANOVA, repANOVA)
G <- df_list3 %>% reduce(full_join, by = c("Model", "paper_ID", "n", "term"))
G <- G %>%
    mutate( O_pN = as.numeric(O_p),
             R_pN = R_p,
              R_p = as.character(R_p),
            R_p = as.character(R_p))

# Rename p-values for consistency
CX <- C %>% rename("O_p.value" = "O_p", "R_p.value" = "R_p", "O_p.valueN" = "O_pN", "R_p.valueN" = "R_pN")
FX <- F %>% rename("O_p_value" = "O_p", "R_p_value" = "R_p", "O_p_valueN" = "O_pN", "R_p_valueN" = "R_pN")
GX <- G %>% rename("O_pvalue" = "O_p", "R_pvalue" = "R_p", "O_pvalueN" = "O_pN", "R_pvalueN" = "R_pN")
df_list30 <- list(CX, FX)
fdat <- df_list30%>% reduce(full_join, by = c("Model", "paper_ID", "n", "term"))



decimal_places <- function(x) {
  if (is.na(x)) {
    return(NA)
  }
  # Ensure x is treated as a string and count the decimals even if the value is 0.000
  x_str <- as.character(x)
  
  if (grepl("\\.", x_str)) {
    # Count characters after the decimal point
    return(nchar(strsplit(x_str, ".", fixed = TRUE)[[1]][[2]]))
  } else {
    return(0)  # If there's no decimal point, return 0
  }
}

# Function to compare absolute difference to rounding error with three categories
compare_diff_to_rounding_error <- function(original_num, reproduced_num, dec_places) {
  if (is.na(original_num) || is.na(reproduced_num) || is.na(dec_places)) {
    return(list(category = NA, rounding_error_threshold_1 = NA, rounding_error_threshold_2 = NA))
  }
  
  rounding_error_threshold_1 <- 0.50 * 10^(-dec_places)
  rounding_error_threshold_2 <- 0.999 * 10^(-dec_places)
  absolute_diff <- abs(original_num - reproduced_num)
  
  category <- if (absolute_diff <= rounding_error_threshold_1) {
    "Reproduced"
  } else if (absolute_diff > rounding_error_threshold_1 && absolute_diff <= rounding_error_threshold_2) {
    "Incorrect Rounding"
  } else {
    "Not Reproduced"
  }
  
  return(list(category = category, rounding_error_threshold_1 = rounding_error_threshold_1, rounding_error_threshold_2 = rounding_error_threshold_2))
}

# List of comparisons to perform
comparisons <- list(
  list(o_col_cat = "O_est", o_col_num = "O_estN", r_col = "R_estN"),
  list(o_col_cat = "O_lower", o_col_num = "O_lowerN", r_col = "R_lowerN"), 
  list(o_col_cat = "O_upper", o_col_num = "O_upperN", r_col = "R_upperN"),
  list(o_col_cat = "O_se", o_col_num = "O_seN", r_col = "R_seN"),
  list(o_col_cat = "O_t", o_col_num = "O_tN", r_col = "R_tN"),
  list(o_col_cat = "O_p.value", o_col_num = "O_p.valueN", r_col = "R_p.valueN"),
  list(o_col_cat = "O_r2", o_col_num = "O_r2N", r_col = "R_r2N"),
  list(o_col_cat = "O_r2Adj", o_col_num = "O_r2AdjN", r_col = "R_r2AdjN"),
  list(o_col_cat = "O_f", o_col_num = "O_fN", r_col = "R_fN")
)

# Applying the comparisons
for (comp in comparisons) {
  o_col_cat <- comp$o_col_cat
  o_col_num <- comp$o_col_num
  r_col <- comp$r_col
  
  # Create dynamic column names
  category_col <- paste0("category_", o_col_cat)
  threshold_1_col <- paste0("rounding_error_threshold_1_", o_col_cat)
  threshold_2_col <- paste0("rounding_error_threshold_2_", o_col_cat)
  
  # Apply the function and unnest the result
  fdat <- fdat %>%
    rowwise() %>%
    mutate(dec_places = decimal_places(!!sym(o_col_cat))) %>%
    mutate(result = list(compare_diff_to_rounding_error(!!sym(o_col_num), !!sym(r_col), dec_places))) %>%
    unnest_wider(result, names_sep = paste0("_", o_col_cat, "_")) %>%
    ungroup() %>%
    select(-dec_places)
}



# Reshape est
est_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, changeB, change.lci, change.uci,change.se, changep, SigChange, SigChangeDirection,
         changeR2,Change.f, Change.df1, Change.df2, Reproduce.df1, Reproduce.df2,
          result_O_est_category, result_O_est_rounding_error_threshold_1, result_O_est_rounding_error_threshold_2,
          result_O_lower_category, result_O_lower_rounding_error_threshold_1, result_O_lower_rounding_error_threshold_2, 
          result_O_upper_category, result_O_upper_rounding_error_threshold_1, result_O_upper_rounding_error_threshold_2,
         result_O_se_category, result_O_se_rounding_error_threshold_1, result_O_se_rounding_error_threshold_2,
         result_O_t_category, result_O_t_rounding_error_threshold_1, result_O_t_rounding_error_threshold_2,
          result_O_r2_category, result_O_r2_rounding_error_threshold_1, result_O_r2_rounding_error_threshold_2,
         result_O_r2Adj_category, result_O_r2Adj_rounding_error_threshold_1, result_O_r2Adj_rounding_error_threshold_2,
          result_O_f_category, result_O_f_rounding_error_threshold_1, result_O_f_rounding_error_threshold_2,
           O_est, R_est) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_est, R_est),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 'est'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_est", "Original", "Reproduced"))

estN_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, O_estN, R_estN) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_estN, R_estN),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 'estN'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_estN", "Original", "Reproduced"))


# Reshape lower
lower_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, O_lower, R_lower) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_lower, R_lower),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 'lower'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_lower", "Original", "Reproduced"))

# Reshape lowerN
lowerN_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, O_lowerN, R_lowerN) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_lowerN, R_lowerN),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 'lowerN'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_lowerN", "Original", "Reproduced"))

# Reshape upper
upper_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, O_upper, R_upper) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_upper, R_upper),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 'upper'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_upper", "Original", "Reproduced"))

# Reshape upperN
upperN_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, O_upperN, R_upperN) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_upperN, R_upperN),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 'upperN'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_upperN", "Original", "Reproduced"))


# Reshape se
se_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, O_se, R_se) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_se, R_se),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 'se'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_se", "Original", "Reproduced"))

# Reshape se
seN_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, O_seN, R_seN) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_seN, R_seN),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 'seN'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_seN", "Original", "Reproduced"))


# Reshape t
t_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, O_t, R_t) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_t, R_t),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 't'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_t", "Original", "Reproduced"))

# Reshape t
tN_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, O_tN, R_tN) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_tN, R_tN),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 'tN'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_tN", "Original", "Reproduced"))

# Reshape stdEst
stdEst_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, O_stdEst, R_stdEst) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_stdEst, R_stdEst),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 'stdEst'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_stdEst", "Original", "Reproduced"))

# Reshape stdEstLower
stdEstLower_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, O_stdEstLower, R_stdEstLower) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_stdEstLower, R_stdEstLower),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 'stdEstLower'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_stdEstLower", "Original", "Reproduced"))

# Reshape stdEstUpper
stdEstUpper_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, O_stdEstUpper, R_stdEstUpper) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_stdEstUpper, R_stdEstUpper),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 'stdEstUpper'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_stdEstUpper", "Original", "Reproduced"))

# Reshape r
r_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, O_r, R_r) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_r, R_r),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 'r'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_r", "Original", "Reproduced"))

# make numeric
fdat$R_r2<-as.numeric(fdat$R_r2)
fdat$O_r2<-as.numeric(fdat$O_r2)
```
