```{r}
# Reshape r2
r2_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, O_r2, R_r2) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_r2, R_r2),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 'r2'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_r2", "Original", "Reproduced"))


# Reshape r2Adj
r2Adj_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, O_r2Adj, R_r2Adj) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_r2Adj, R_r2Adj),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 'r2Adj'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_r2Adj", "Original", "Reproduced"))

# Reshape r2Adj
r2AdjN_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, O_r2AdjN, R_r2AdjN) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_r2AdjN, R_r2AdjN),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 'r2AdjN'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_r2AdjN", "Original", "Reproduced"))


# Reshape aic
aic_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, O_aic, R_aic) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_aic, R_aic),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 'aic'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_aic", "Original", "Reproduced"))

# Reshape rmse
rmse_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, O_rmse, R_rmse) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_rmse, R_rmse),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 'rmse'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_rmse", "Original", "Reproduced"))

# Reshape f
f_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, O_f, R_f) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_f, R_f),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 'f'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_f", "Original", "Reproduced"))

# Reshape df1
df1_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, O_df1, R_df1) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_df1, R_df1),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 'df1'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_df1", "Original", "Reproduced"))


# Reshape df2
df2_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, O_df2, R_df2) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_df2, R_df2),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 'df2'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_df2", "Original", "Reproduced"))


# Reshape p.value
p.value_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, O_p.value, R_p.value) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_p.value, R_p.value),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 'p.value'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_p.value", "Original", "Reproduced"))

# Reshape p.value
p.valueN_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, O_p.valueN, R_p.valueN) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_p.valueN, R_p.valueN),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 'p.valueN'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_p.valueN", "Original", "Reproduced"))


# Reshape p_value
p_value_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, O_p_value, R_p_value) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_p_value, R_p_value),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 'p_value'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_p_value", "Original", "Reproduced"))

# Reshape p_value
p_valueN_long <- fdat %>%
  select(paper_ID, Model, term, n, O_p_valueN, R_p_valueN) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(O_p_valueN, R_p_valueN),
    names_to = 'Source',
    values_to = 'p_valueN'
  ) %>%
  mutate(Source = ifelse(Source == "O_p_valueN", "Original", "Reproduced"))


# Initialize the final dataset with est_long
ffdat <- est_long


# Merge each reshaped dataset one by one
ffdat <- ffdat %>%
  full_join(estN_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(lower_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(lowerN_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(upper_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(upperN_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(se_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(seN_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(t_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(tN_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(stdEst_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(stdEstLower_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(stdEstUpper_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(r_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>%  
  full_join(r2_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(r2Adj_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(r2AdjN_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(aic_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(rmse_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(f_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(df1_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
  full_join(df2_long,by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
 
  
 
 full_join(p_value_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
 full_join(p_valueN_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 

 full_join(p.value_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) %>% 
 full_join(p.valueN_long, by = c("paper_ID", "Model", "term", "n", "Source")) 





# Assign final datasets to the global environment
assign_df("origCoef", origCoef)
assign_df("repCoef", repCoef)
assign_df("fit_orig", origfit)
assign_df("fit_rep", repfit)
assign_df("anova_orig", origANOVA)
assign_df("anova_rep", repANOVA)
assign_df("fdat", fdat)
assign_df("ffdat", ffdat)



```

```{r}
# Function to dynamically assign data frames to the global environment
assign_df <- function(name, df) {
  assign(name, df, envir = .GlobalEnv)
}

# Function to dynamically fetch a data frame
get_dynamic_df <- function(df_name) {
  get(df_name, envir = .GlobalEnv)
}

# Get the unique model numbers
model_numbers <- unique(fdat$Model)

# Split the fdat data frame by Model and assign each subset to the global environment
split_fdat <- split(fdat, fdat$Model)

# Dynamically assign each split dataset to the global environment
for (i in seq_along(model_numbers)) {
  model_number <- model_numbers[i]
  assign_df(paste0("fdat", model_number), split_fdat[[i]])
}



# Function to generate a forest plot
generate_forest_plot <- function(model_num) {
  original_data <- get_dynamic_df(paste0("O_coefficients_raw_df_", model_num)) %>%
    dplyr::select(n, term, est, lower, upper) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(Source = "Original") %>%
    dplyr::mutate(est = as.numeric(est), lower = as.numeric(lower), upper = as.numeric(upper)) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(Order = 0)  # Set Order to 0 for Original
  
  reproduced_data <- get_dynamic_df(paste0("R_coefficients_raw_df_", model_num)) %>%
    dplyr::select(n, term, est, lower, upper) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(Source = "Reproduced") %>%
    dplyr::mutate(est = as.numeric(est), lower = as.numeric(lower), upper = as.numeric(upper)) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(Order = 1)  # Set Order to 1 for Reproduced
  
  combined_data <- dplyr::bind_rows(original_data, reproduced_data) %>%
    dplyr::filter(term != "Intercept") %>%
    dplyr::mutate(OrderHelper = n + Order / 1000) %>%
    dplyr::arrange(OrderHelper) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(Label = paste(term, Source, sep = " - ")) %>%
    dplyr::arrange(desc(OrderHelper))  # Reverse the order of the data
  
  # Set the factor levels based on reversed order
  combined_data$Label <- factor(combined_data$Label, levels = combined_data$Label)
  
  
  plot <- ggplot2::ggplot(combined_data, ggplot2::aes(x = est, y = Label)) +
    ggplot2::geom_errorbar(ggplot2::aes(xmin = lower, xmax = upper, color = Source), width = 0.2) +
    ggplot2::geom_point(ggplot2::aes(color = Source), size = 3) +
    ggplot2::scale_color_manual(values = c("Original" = "darkblue", "Reproduced" = "darkred")) +
    ggplot2::labs(x = "Coefficient", y = "") +
    ggplot2::theme_minimal() +
    ggplot2::theme(
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      panel.background = element_blank(),
      plot.background = ggplot2::element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
      legend.position = "top",
      axis.line = ggplot2::element_line(color = "black")
    )
  
  ggplot2::ggsave(paste0("figures/forest_plot_model_", model_num, ".png"), plot = plot)
}


# Function to generate Bland-Altman plot using blandr with error handling
generate_bland_altman_plot <- function(fdat, model_num) {
  tryCatch({
    # Perform Bland-Altman analysis to get statistics
    blandr_results <- blandr::blandr.statistics(
      method1 = fdat$R_p.valueN,
      method2 = fdat$O_p.valueN
    )
    
    # Generate the Bland-Altman plot without default annotations
    plot <- blandr::blandr.draw(
      fdat$R_p.valueN, 
      fdat$O_p.valueN, 
      ciDisplay = FALSE, 
      ciShading = FALSE,
      plotTitle = paste("")
    )
    
    # Add custom annotations with text above the lines, rounded to three decimals
    plot <- plot +
      ggplot2::geom_hline(yintercept = blandr_results$bias, color = "blue", linetype = "dashed") +
      ggplot2::geom_hline(yintercept = blandr_results$upperLOA, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
      ggplot2::geom_hline(yintercept = blandr_results$lowerLOA, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
      ggplot2::annotate("text", x = max(fdat$R_p.valueN, na.rm = TRUE), y = blandr_results$bias + 0.01, 
                        label = paste("Mean Bias:", Sround(blandr_results$bias, 3)), vjust = -0.5, hjust = 1) +
      ggplot2::annotate("text", x = max(fdat$R_p.valueN, na.rm = TRUE), y = blandr_results$upperLOA + 0.01, 
                        label = paste("Upper LoA:", Sround(blandr_results$upperLOA, 3)), vjust = -0.5, hjust = 1) +
      ggplot2::annotate("text", x = max(fdat$R_p.valueN, na.rm = TRUE), y = blandr_results$lowerLOA + 0.01, 
                        label = paste("Lower LoA:", Sround(blandr_results$lowerLOA, 3)), vjust = -0.5, hjust = 1) +
      ggplot2::labs(
        x = "Mean of Reproduced and Original",
        y = "Difference (Reproduced - Original)"
      ) +
      ggplot2::theme_minimal() +
      ggplot2::theme(
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.background = element_blank(),
        plot.background = ggplot2::element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
        panel.border = ggplot2::element_rect(color = "black", fill = NA, size = 1),
        axis.text = ggplot2::element_text(size = 12),
        axis.title = ggplot2::element_text(size = 14),
        plot.title = ggplot2::element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 16)
      )
    
    # Save the plot
    ggplot2::ggsave(filename = paste0("figures/bland_altman_plot_model_", model_num, ".png"), plot = plot, width = 7.74, height = 5.47)
  }, error = function(e) {
    message(paste("Bland-Altman plot failed for model", model_num, ": ", e$message))
  })
}


# Function to create and assign the CC table
create_cc_table <- function(fdat, model_num) {
  CC <- fdat %>%
    dplyr::select(term, O_est, R_estN, changeB, result_O_est_category) %>%
    mutate(across(c(changeB), ~ Sround(.x, thresround))) %>% 
    mutate(changeB = ifelse(changeB=="NA", "", changeB)) %>% 
    # mutate(O_estN = ifelse(O_estN=="NA", "", O_estN)) %>%
     mutate(R_estN = ifelse(R_estN=="NA", "", R_estN)) %>%
    flextable::flextable() %>%
    flextable::align(align = "left", part = "all") %>%
    flextable::width(j = "term", width = 2) %>%
    flextable::set_header_labels(O_est = "O_B", R_estN = "R_B", changeB = "Change.B", result_O_est_category = "reproduce.B") %>%
    flextable::add_footer_lines("O_B = original B; R_B = reproduced B; Change.B = change in R_B - O_B; Reproduce.B = B reproduced.")
  
  assign_df(paste0("CC_model_", model_num), CC)
}

# Function to create and assign the CL table
create_cl_table <- function(fdat, model_num) {
  CL <- fdat %>%
    dplyr::select(term, O_lower, R_lowerN, change.lci, result_O_lower_category) %>%
    mutate(across(c(change.lci), ~ Sround(.x, thresround))) %>% 
    mutate(change.lci = ifelse(change.lci=="NA", "", change.lci)) %>% 
    mutate(R_lowerN = ifelse(R_lowerN=="NA", "", R_lowerN)) %>%
    flextable::flextable() %>%
    flextable::align(align = "left", part = "all") %>%
    flextable::width(j = "term", width = 2) %>%
    flextable::set_header_labels(O_lower= "O_lower", R_lowerN= "R_lower",  change.lci = "Change.lci", result_O_lower_category = "Reproduce.lower") %>%
    flextable::add_footer_lines("O_lower = original lower confidence interval; R_lower = reproduced lower confidence interval; change.lci = change in R_lower - O_lower; Reproduce.lower = lower confidence interval reproduced.")
  
  assign_df(paste0("CL_model_", model_num), CL)
}

# Function to create and assign the CU table
create_cu_table <- function(fdat, model_num) {
  CU <- fdat %>%
    dplyr::select(term, O_upper, R_upperN, change.uci, result_O_upper_category) %>%
     mutate(across(c(change.uci), ~ Sround(.x, thresround))) %>% 
     mutate(change.uci = ifelse(change.uci=="NA", "", change.uci)) %>% 
    mutate(R_upperN = ifelse(R_upperN=="NA", "", R_upperN)) %>%
    flextable::flextable() %>%
    flextable::align(align = "left", part = "all") %>%
    flextable::width(j = "term", width = 2) %>%
    flextable::set_header_labels(O_upper= "O_upper", R_upperN= "R_upper", change.uci = "Change.uci", result_O_upper_category = "Reproduce.upper") %>%
    flextable::add_footer_lines("O_upper = original upper confidence interval; R_upper = reproduced upper confidence interval; change.uci = change in R_upper - O_upper; Reproduce.upper = upper confidence interval reproduced.")
  
  assign_df(paste0("CU_model_", model_num), CU)
}


# Function to create and assign the CL table
create_se_table <- function(fdat, model_num) {
  SE <- fdat %>%
    dplyr::select(term, O_seN, R_seN, change.se, result_O_se_category) %>%
    mutate(across(c(R_seN, change.se), ~ Sround(.x, thresround))) %>% 
    mutate(change.se = ifelse(change.se=="NA", "", change.se)) %>% 
    mutate(R_seN = ifelse(R_seN=="NA", "", R_seN)) %>% 
    flextable::flextable() %>%
    flextable::align(align = "left", part = "all") %>%
    flextable::width(j = "result_O_se_category", width = 2.5) %>%
    flextable::set_header_labels(O_seN= "O_SE", R_seN= "R_SE",  change.se = "Change.SE", result_O_se_category = "Reproduce.SE") %>%
    flextable::add_footer_lines("O_SE = original standard error; R_SE = reproduced standard error; Change.SE = change in R_SE - O_SE; Reproduce.lower = standard error reproduced.")
  
  assign_df(paste0("SE_model_", model_num), SE)
}

# Function to create and assign the CL table
create_t_table <- function(fdat, model_num) {
  t <- fdat %>%
    dplyr::select(term, O_tN, R_tN, change.t, result_O_t_category) %>%
    mutate(across(c(R_tN, change.t), ~ Sround(.x, thresround))) %>% 
    mutate(change.t = ifelse(change.t =="NA", "", change.t)) %>% 
    mutate(R_tN = ifelse(R_tN=="NA", "", R_tN)) %>% 
    flextable::flextable() %>%
    flextable::align(align = "left", part = "all") %>%
    flextable::width(j = "result_O_t_category", width = 2.5) %>%
    flextable::set_header_labels(O_tN= "O_t", R_tN= "R_t",  change.t = "Change.t", result_O_t_category = "Reproduce.t") %>%
    flextable::add_footer_lines("O_t = original t value; R_t = reproduced t value; Change.t = change in R_t - O_t; Reproduce.lower = t value reproduced.")
  
  assign_df(paste0("t_model_", model_num), t)
}


# Function to create and assign the FF table
create_r2_table <- function(fdat, model_num) {
  R2 <- fdat %>%
    dplyr::filter(n == 1) %>%
    dplyr::select(O_r2N, R_r2N, changeR2, result_O_r2_category) %>%
    mutate(across(c(R_r2N, changeR2), ~ Sround(.x, thresround))) %>% 
    mutate(O_r2N = ifelse(O_r2N =="NA", "", O_r2N)) %>% 
      mutate(changeR2 = ifelse(changeR2=="NA", "", changeR2)) %>% 
    flextable::flextable() %>%
    compose(i = 1, j = "O_r2N", part = "header", value = as_paragraph("O_R", as_sup("2"))) %>%
    compose(i = 1, j = "R_r2N", part = "header", value = as_paragraph("R_R", as_sup("2"))) %>%
    compose(i = 1, j = "changeR2", part = "header", value = as_paragraph("Change.R", as_sup("2"))) %>%
    compose(i = 1, j = "result_O_r2_category", part = "header", value = as_paragraph("Reproduce.R", as_sup("2"))) %>%
    flextable::align(align = "left", part = "all") %>%
    flextable::width(j = "result_O_r2_category", width = 2.5) %>%
    flextable::  colformat_double(digits = 3) %>%
    flextable::add_footer_lines(
  as_paragraph(
    "O_R", as_sup("2"), " = original R", as_sup("2"), 
    "; R_R", as_sup("2"), " = reproduced R", as_sup("2"), 
    "; Change.R", as_sup("2"), " = change in R_R", as_sup("2"), " - O_R", as_sup("2")
  )
)
  
  assign_df(paste0("R2_model_", model_num),  R2)
}


# Function to create and assign the FF table
create_r2Adj_table <- function(fdat, model_num) {
  R2Adj <- fdat %>%
    dplyr::filter(n == 1) %>%
    dplyr::select(O_r2AdjN, R_r2AdjN, changeR2Adj, result_O_r2Adj_category) %>%
    mutate(across(c(R_r2AdjN, changeR2Adj), ~ Sround(.x, thresround))) %>% 
    mutate(O_r2AdjN = ifelse(O_r2AdjN =="NA", "", O_r2AdjN)) %>% 
      mutate(changeR2Adj = ifelse(changeR2Adj=="NA", "", changeR2Adj)) %>% 
    flextable::flextable() %>%
    compose(i = 1, j = "O_r2AdjN", part = "header", value = as_paragraph("O_R", as_sup("2"), "Adj")) %>%
    compose(i = 1, j = "R_r2AdjN", part = "header", value = as_paragraph("R_R", as_sup("2"), "Adj")) %>%
    compose(i = 1, j = "changeR2Adj", part = "header", value = as_paragraph("Change.R", as_sup("2"), "Adj")) %>%
    compose(i = 1, j = "result_O_r2Adj_category", part = "header", value = as_paragraph("Reproduce.R", as_sup("2"), "Adj")) %>%
    flextable::align(align = "left", part = "all") %>%
    flextable::width(j = "result_O_r2Adj_category", width = 2.5) %>%
    flextable::  colformat_double(digits = 3) %>%
    flextable::add_footer_lines(
        as_paragraph(
            "O_R", as_sup("2"), "Adj = original R", as_sup("2"), " Adjusted", 
            "; R_R", as_sup("2"), "Adj = reproduced R", as_sup("2"), " Adjusted", 
            "; Change.R", as_sup("2"), "Adj = change in R", as_sup("2"), "Adj (R", as_sup("2"), "Adj  - O_R", as_sup("2"), "Adj)",
            "; Reproduce.R", as_sup("2"), "Adj = R", as_sup("2"), " Adjusted reproduced."
        )
    )
  assign_df(paste0("R2Adj_model_", model_num),  R2Adj)
}

# Function to create and assign the DF table
create_df_table <- function(fdat, model_num) {
  DF <- fdat %>%
    dplyr::filter(n == 1) %>%
    dplyr::select(O_df1, R_df1, Change.df1, Reproduce.df1, O_df2, R_df2, Change.df2, Reproduce.df2) %>%
    flextable::flextable() %>%
    flextable::align(align = "left", part = "all") %>%
    flextable::set_header_labels(O_df1 = "O_DF1", R_df1 = "R_DF1", Change.df1 = "Change.DF1", Reproduce.df1 = "Reproduce.DF1", O_df2 = "O_DF2", R_df2 = "R_DF2", Change.df2 = "Change.DF2", Reproduce.df2 = "Reproduce.DF2") %>%
    flextable::add_footer_lines("O_DF1 = original degrees of freedom for the model; R_DF1 = reproduced degrees of freedom for the model; Change.DF1 = change in R_DF1 - O_DF1; Reproduce.DF1 = reproduced degrees of freedom for the model (R_DF1 = O_DF1); O_DF2 = original degrees of freedom for the residuals; R_DF2 = reproduced degrees of freedom for the residuals; Change.DF2 = change in R_DF2 - O_DF2; Reproduce.DF2 = reproduced degrees of freedom for the residuals (R_DF2 = O_DF2).")
  
  assign_df(paste0("DF_model_", model_num), DF)
}

create_cp_table <- function(fdat, model_num) {
  Cp <- fdat %>%
    dplyr::select(term, O_p.value, R_p.valueN, changep, result_O_p.value_category, SigChangeDirection) %>%
    mutate(changep = Sround(changep, 4)) %>%
    mutate(R_p.valueN = sapply(R_p.valueN, round_p)) %>%
    mutate(O_p.value = ifelse(O_p.value == "NA", "", O_p.value)) %>%
    mutate(changep = ifelse(changep == "NA", "", changep)) %>%
    flextable::flextable() %>%
    flextable::align(align = "left", part = "all") %>%
    flextable::width(j = "SigChangeDirection", width = 3) %>%
    flextable::set_header_labels(term = "Term",  O_p.value = "O_p", R_p.valueN="R_p", changep = "Change.p", result_O_p.value_category = "Reproduce.p") %>%
    flextable::add_footer_lines("O_p = original p-value; R_p = reproduced p-value; Changep = change in p-value R_p - O_p; Reproduce.p = p-values reproduced. SigChangeDirection = statistical significance and B change between original and reproduced models. Note, p-values that were <0.001 were set to 0.00099 for the purposes of comparison.") %>%
    flextable::  colformat_double(digits = 3)
  
  assign_df(paste0("Cp_model_", model_num), Cp)
}


 #Function to create and assign the FF table
create_ff_table <- function(fdat, model_num) {
  FF <- fdat %>%
    dplyr::filter(n == 1) %>%
    dplyr::select(term, O_f, R_fN, Change.f, result_O_f_category) %>%
    mutate(across(c(R_fN, Change.f), ~ Sround(.x, thresround))) %>% 
    mutate(Change.f = ifelse(Change.f=="NA", "", Change.f)) %>% 
    mutate(R_fN = ifelse(R_fN =="NA", "", R_fN)) %>% 
    flextable::flextable() %>%
    flextable::align(align = "left", part = "all") %>%
    flextable::width(j = "result_O_f_category", width = 2.5) %>%
    flextable::set_header_labels(
      term = "Term",
      O_f = "O_F",
      R_fN = "R_F",
      Change.f = "Change.F",
      result_O_f_category = "Reproduce.F"
    ) %>%
    flextable::add_footer_lines("O_F = original global F; R_F = reproduced global F; Change.F = change in R_F - O_F; Reproduce.F = Global F reproduced.")
  
  assign_df(paste0("FF_model_", model_num), FF)
}




# Generate model outputs
generate_model_outputs <- function(model_num) {
  generate_forest_plot(model_num)
  fdat <- get_dynamic_df(paste0("fdat", model_num))
  generate_bland_altman_plot(fdat, model_num)
  create_cc_table(fdat, model_num)
  create_cl_table(fdat, model_num)
  create_cu_table(fdat, model_num)
  create_se_table(fdat, model_num)
  create_t_table(fdat, model_num)
  create_r2_table(fdat, model_num)
  create_r2Adj_table(fdat, model_num)
  create_ff_table(fdat, model_num)
  create_df_table(fdat, model_num)
  create_cp_table(fdat, model_num)
}

# Example list of model numbers (you can define this based on your actual models)
model_numbers <- 1:num_models

# Call the function for each model
for (i in model_numbers) {
  generate_model_outputs(i)
}


```

```{r M1Rassump}

R_description_raw1 <- R_description_flextable_1$body$dataset


# Create an empty dataset for assumptions
M1Rassump<- data.frame(matrix(nrow = nrow(O_anova_raw_df_1), ncol = 25))
# Assign column names
names(M1Rassump) <- c("paper_ID",  "Model", "origin", "n", "Term", "linearplot", "linear",  "homtest", "homplot", "hom",  "VIF", "KS", "SW", "QQ", "normal", 
                      "collin", "indDes", "indtest", "indep", "R2", "cooks", "DFFIT", "DFbeta", "CovRatio", "Outlier") 


M1Rassump<-M1Rassump %>% mutate(Model=1, origin="Reproduced", n=1:nrow(.))
M1Rassump$paper_ID <- paper_ID
M1lintest<-R_residuals_raw_df_1 %>% dplyr::select(n, Model, paper_ID,origin, Results) %>% rename("lineartest"="Results") %>% 
  mutate(lineartest= case_when(lineartest== "Linearity violation" ~ "Violation", lineartest==  "No linearity violation"~ "No Violation", TRUE ~ NA_character_))
M1Rassump <-merge(M1Rassump, M1lintest, by=c("paper_ID", "Model", "origin", "n"))
M1colin<- R_collinearity_raw_df_1 %>%  add_row(.before = 1) %>% mutate(Model = 1, paper_ID = paper_ID, origin = "Reproduced", n=1:nrow(O_anova_raw_df_1)) %>% 
  dplyr::select(vif, n, Model, paper_ID,origin) 
M1colin$paper_ID <- paper_ID
M1Rassump <-merge(M1Rassump, M1colin, by=c("paper_ID", "Model", "origin", "n"))
M1Rassump$Term <- O_anova_raw_df_1$term[order(O_anova_raw_df_1$n)]

# note some variables on a overall model level some at variable level, if this is the case n=1 is the overall model for example linearity. DFBeta is measured at coeffcient level but has been made to model level.
M1Rassump<-M1Rassump %>%
mutate(linear= case_when(n==1 | n==2   ~ "No Violation")) %>%   # not automatic based on overall information  
mutate(homtest = ifelse(R_homoscedasticity_raw_df_1$p.value < 0.05, "Violation", "No Violation")) %>% 
mutate(homplot = "Violation", hom="Violation") %>% # not automatic
mutate(linearplot= case_when(n==1 | n==2   ~ "No Violation")) %>% # not automatic
mutate(KS = ifelse(R_normality_ks_raw_df_1$p.value < 0.05, "Violation", "No Violation")) %>% 
mutate(SW = ifelse(R_normality_sw_raw_df_1$p.value < 0.05, "Violation", "No Violation")) %>% 
mutate(QQ = "No Violation", normal="No Violation") %>% # not automatic
mutate(indtest = ifelse(R_independence_raw_df_1$p < 0.05, "Violation", "No Violation")) %>% 
mutate(VIF = ifelse(vif < 5, "No Violation", "Violation")) %>% 
mutate(collin = "NA") %>%  # not automatic based on overall information, including assessing SE if problem
mutate(indDes = "No Violation", indep="No Violation") %>% # not automatic
dplyr::select(-vif) %>%  
mutate(R2 = "Correct") 


DFbeta_table1 <- R_description_raw1 %>%
  filter(str_starts(term, "dfb.")) %>%
  mutate(
    Term = str_remove(term, "^dfb\\."),
    Term = if_else(Term == "1_", "Intercept", Term),
    DFbeta = if_else(min <= -1 | max >= 1, "Yes", "No")
  )%>% dplyr::select(Term, DFbeta)
DFbeta_table1$Term<-R_coefficients_raw_df_1$term[!is.na(R_coefficients_raw_df_1$est)]



M1Rassump$DFbeta <- if_else(
  any(DFbeta_table1$DFbeta == "Yes", na.rm = TRUE),
  "Yes",
  "No"
)

M1Rassump <- M1Rassump |>
  mutate(
    cooks = if_else(flag_any(R_description_raw1, ".cooksd", max >= 0.5),
                    "Yes", "No")) |>
  mutate(DFFIT = if_else(
      flag_any(R_description_raw1, "dffit", min <= -1 | max >= 1),
      "Yes", "No"),
    CovRatio = if_else(
      flag_any(R_description_raw1, "cov.r", min <= 0.9 | max >= 1.1),
      "Yes", "No"))|>
  dplyr::mutate(
    Outlier = if_else(
      any(cooks == "Yes" | DFbeta == "Yes" | DFFIT == "Yes"),
      "Yes", "No")) |> 
  arrange(n)




```

```{r M3Rassump}

R_description_raw3 <- R_description_flextable_3$body$dataset
# Create an empty dataset for assumptions
M3Rassump<- data.frame(matrix(nrow = nrow(O_anova_raw_df_3), ncol = 25))
# Assign column names
names(M3Rassump) <- c("paper_ID",  "Model", "origin", "n", "Term", "linearplot", "linear",  "homtest", "homplot", "hom",  "VIF", "KS", "SW", "QQ", "normal", 
                      "collin", "indDes", "indtest", "indep", "R2", "cooks", "DFFIT", "DFbeta", "CovRatio", "Outlier")


M3Rassump<-M3Rassump %>% mutate(Model=3, origin="Reproduced",  n=1:nrow(.))
M3Rassump$paper_ID <- paper_ID
M3lintest<-R_residuals_raw_df_3 %>% dplyr::select(n, Model, paper_ID,origin, Results) %>% rename("lineartest"="Results") %>% 
  mutate(lineartest= case_when(lineartest== "Linearity violation" ~ "Violation", lineartest==  "No linearity violation"~ "No Violation", TRUE ~ NA_character_))
M3Rassump <-merge(M3Rassump, M3lintest, by=c("paper_ID", "Model", "origin", "n"))
M3colin<- R_collinearity_raw_df_3 %>%  add_row(.before = 1) %>% mutate(Model = 3, paper_ID = paper_ID, origin = "Reproduced", n=1:nrow(O_anova_raw_df_3)) %>% 
  dplyr::select(vif, n, Model, paper_ID,origin) 
M3colin$paper_ID <- paper_ID
M3Rassump <-merge(M3Rassump, M3colin, by=c("paper_ID", "Model", "origin", "n"))
M3Rassump$Term <- O_anova_raw_df_3$term[order(O_anova_raw_df_3$n)]

# note some variables on a overall model level some at variable level, if this is the case n=1 is the overall model for example linearity. DFBeta is measured at coeffcient level but has been made to model level.
M3Rassump<-M3Rassump %>%
mutate(linearplot= case_when(n==1 | n==3  ~ "Violation", n==2 ~ "No Violation")) %>% # not automatic
mutate(linear= case_when(n==1 | n==3  ~ "Violation", n==2 ~ "No Violation")) %>%   # not automatic based on overall information  
mutate(homtest = ifelse(R_homoscedasticity_raw_df_3$p.value < 0.05, "Violation", "No Violation")) %>% 
mutate(homplot = "Violation", hom="Violation") %>% # not automatic
mutate(KS = ifelse(R_normality_ks_raw_df_3$p.value < 0.05, "Violation", "No Violation")) %>% 
mutate(SW = ifelse(R_normality_sw_raw_df_3$p.value < 0.05, "Violation", "No Violation")) %>% 
mutate(QQ = "No Violation", normal="No Violation") %>% # not automatic
mutate(indtest = ifelse(R_independence_raw_df_3$p < 0.05, "Violation", "No Violation")) %>% 
mutate(VIF = ifelse(vif < 5, "No Violation", "Violation")) %>% 
mutate(collin = "No Violation") %>%  # not automatic based on overall information, including assessing SE if problem
mutate(indDes = "No Violation", indep="No Violation") %>% # not automatic
dplyr::select(-vif) %>%  
mutate(R2 = "Incorrect") 

DFbeta_table3 <- R_description_raw3 %>%
  filter(str_starts(term, "dfb.")) %>%
  mutate(
    Term = str_remove(term, "^dfb\\."),
    Term = if_else(Term == "1_", "Intercept", Term),
    DFbeta = if_else(min <= -1 | max >= 1, "Yes", "No")
  )%>% dplyr::select(Term, DFbeta)
DFbeta_table3$Term<-R_coefficients_raw_df_3$term[!is.na(R_coefficients_raw_df_3$est)]



M3Rassump$DFbeta <- if_else(
  any(DFbeta_table3$DFbeta == "Yes", na.rm = TRUE),
  "Yes",
  "No"
)

M3Rassump <- M3Rassump |>
  mutate(
    cooks = if_else(flag_any(R_description_raw3, ".cooksd", max >= 0.5),
                    "Yes", "No")) |>
  mutate(DFFIT = if_else(
      flag_any(R_description_raw3, "dffit", min <= -1 | max >= 1),
      "Yes", "No"),
    CovRatio = if_else(
      flag_any(R_description_raw3, "cov.r", min <= 0.9 | max >= 1.1),
      "Yes", "No")) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    Outlier = if_else(
      any(cooks == "Yes" | DFbeta == "Yes" | DFFIT == "Yes"),
      "Yes", "No")) |> arrange(n)




```

# Model 1

::: panel-tabset
### Original results

##### Model results for `r namey1`

```{r, ft.align="left"}
O_coefficients_flextable_1
```

##### Fit statistics for `r namey1`

```{r, ft.align="left"}
O_fit_stats_flextable_1
```

##### ANOVA table for `r namey1`

```{r, ft.align="left"}
O_anova_flextable_1
```

### Reproduced results

##### Model results for `r namey1`

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_coefficients_flextable_1
```

##### Fit statistics for `r namey1`

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_fit_stats_flextable_1
```

##### ANOVA table for `r namey1`

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_anova_flextable_1

```

##### Visualisation of regression model

The blue line shows the best line of fit with shading representing 95% confidence intervals, while holding all other covariates constant. The dots show partial residuals, which reflect the observed data adjusted for all other predictors except the one being plotted.

```{r, out.width = "100%", out.height="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/reg_plot_1.png') 
```

##### Checking residuals plots for patterns

Blue line showing quadratic fit for residuals

```{r , out.width = "100%", out.height="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/res_plot_1.png') 
```

##### Testing residuals for non linear relationships

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_residuals_flextable_1
```

##### Checking univariate relationships with the dependent variable using scatterplots

Blue line shows linear relationship, red line indicates relationship inferred by GAM modelling

```{r, out.width = "80%", out.height="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/scatter_plot_1.png') 
```

##### Linearity results

The residual and univariate plots show some signs of mild curvature but it was not significant therefore found to be linear.

##### Testing for homoscedasticity

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_homoscedasticity_flextable_1
```

##### Homoscedasticity results

-   `r ifelse(R_homoscedasticity_raw_df_1$p.value[1] < 0.05, "The studentized Breusch-Pagan test indicates heteroscedasticity.", "The studentized Breusch-Pagan test supports homoscedasticity.")`
-   `r somehet`

##### Model descriptives including cook's distance and leverage to understand outliers

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_description_flextable_1

```

##### Cooks threshold

Cook's distance measures the overall change in fit, if the *ith* observation is removed. Potential influential observations are identified by $\text{Cook's Distance}_i > \frac{4}{n}$, where *n* is the number of observations. In practice a threshold of 0.5 to 1 is often used to identify influential observations.

```{r, out.width = "80%", out.height="80%"}

knitr::include_graphics('figures/cooks_threshold_1.png')
```

##### DFFIT threshold

DFFIT measures how many standard deviations the fitted values will change when the *ith* observation is removed. Potential influential observations $\left| \text{DFFITS}_i \right| > \frac{2\sqrt{p}}{\sqrt{n}}$ where *p* is the number of predictors (including the intercept) and *n* is the number of observations. In practice this can result in a large number of points identified, a practical cut-off of 1 was used to flag observations with meaningful impact.

```{r, out.width = "80%", out.height="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/dffit_threshold_1.png')

```

##### DFBETA threshold

DFBETAS quantify the influence of the *ith* observation on the *jth* regression coefficient as the change in that coefficient when the observation is omitted, expressed in units of the coefficient’s estimated standard error. There is a DFBETA for each parameter in the model. Potential influential observations $|\text{DFBETA}_{ij}| > \frac{2}{\sqrt{n}}$, where *n* is the number of observations. In larger datasets, this threshold can flag a high number of observations with only minor influence on the model. A practical cut-off of 1 was used to flag observations with meaningful impact.

```{r, out.width = "100%", out.height="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/dbetas_threshold_1.png') 

```

##### Influence plot

Observations with high leverage (horizontal) and large residuals (vertical, typically at ±2 or ±3 studentized residuals) are concerning, as they may disproportionately influence the model. This combination is reflected by large bubbles with high Cook's distance indicated by darker shadings of blue.

```{r, out.width = "100%", out.height="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/cooks_plots_1.png') 

```

##### COVRATIO plot

COVRATIO measures the overall change in the precision (covariance matrix) of the estimated regression coefficients when the ith observation is removed. Values close to 1 indicate little influence on the model’s precision. Values below 1 suggest that an observation inflates the variances and reduces precision, resulting in wider confidence intervals, whereas values above 1 suggest deflated variances and narrower confidence intervals. A commonly cited guideline is $\left|\mathrm{COVRATIO}_i - 1\right| > \frac{3p}{n}$, where *p* is the number of parameters and *n* is the number of observations. A practical cut-off between 0.9 to 1.1 was used to flag observations with meaningful impact on precision, although there is no agreed universal alternative cut-off.

```{r, out.width = "100%", out.height="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/covratio_plot_1.png') 

```

##### Observations of interest identified by the influence plot

```{r, ft.align="left"}

 R_cooks_distance_flextable_1
```

##### Results for outliers and influential points

One observation had a studentized residual greater than 3, indicating a small outlier; however, this value remained within conventional thresholds for Cook's distance, DFBETA and DFFit. The COVRATIO indicated observations that may affect confidence intervals widths.

##### Checking for normality of the residuals using a Q–Q plot

```{r, out.width = "80%", out.height="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/qq_plot_1.png') 
```

##### Normality of residuals using Shapiro-Wilk and Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_normality_ks_flextable_1
```

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_normality_sw_flextable_1
```

##### Normality results

-   `r ifelse(R_normality_ks_raw_df_1$p.value[1] < 0.05, "The Kolmogorov-Smirnov test indicates residuals may not be normally distributed.", "The Kolmogorov-Smirnov supports residuals being normally distributed.")`
-   `r ifelse(R_normality_sw_raw_df_1$p.value[1] < 0.05, "The Shapiro-Wilk normality test indicates residuals may not be normally distributed.", "The Shapiro-Wilk supports residuals being normally distributed.")`
-   `r normalQ`

##### Assessing independence with the Durbin–Watson test for autocorrelation

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_independence_flextable_1
```

##### Independence results

-   `r ifelse(R_independence_raw_df_1$p[1] < 0.05, "The Durbin–Watson test suggests there are auto-correlation issues.", "The Durbin–Watson test suggests there are no auto-correlation issues.")`
-   `r indDesYes`

##### Assumption conclusions

No meaningful departures from the assumptions of linearity and independence were observed. The Breusch-Pagan test was statistically significant and the residuals vs. fitted plot showed a funnel shape, suggesting heteroscedasticity and a sensitivity analysis using weighted or robust regression is recommended. Normality tests and the Q-Q plot indicated the residuals were approximately normally distributed. Outlier diagnostics indicated that point estimates were unlikely to be substantially affected by influential points, but confidence-interval width could be affected and should be further investigated.

### Differences

##### Forest plot showing original and reproduced coefficients and 95% confidence intervals for `r namey1`

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/forest_plot_model_1.png')

```

##### Change in regression coefficients

```{r, ft.align="left"}

CC_model_1
```

##### Change in R^2^

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R2_model_1

```

##### Change in p-values

```{r, ft.align="left"}
Cp_model_1
```

##### Results for p-values

The p-value was reproduced.

#### Conclusion computational reproducibility

`r CompRep`

### Sensitivity analysis

#### Methods

The model was successfully reproduced; however, there were indications that the residuals may exhibit slight heteroscedasticity. To further verify the findings, bootstrapped standardized regression coefficients and their 95% confidence intervals were examined. Percentage and absolute changes in estimates and confidence-interval bounds relative to the linear model were summarised using thresholds of 10% change and standardized coefficient differences of \<0.10 and \<0.20. Coefficient direction and statistical significance were assessed for consistency. Wild bootstrapping was used to account for potential heteroscedasticity.

#### Results

```{r}

dat <- dat %>%  
  mutate(across(c(Funding_per_100000, DALY_per_100000, Citations, Average_GDP_PC), ~ as.numeric(scale(.)), .names = "z_{.col}"))  %>%  mutate(z_Citations2=z_Citations^2)%>%
 mutate(z_Average_GDP_PC2=z_Average_GDP_PC^2) %>%
   mutate(z_Average_GDP_PC3=z_Average_GDP_PC^3)



model_ols <- lm(z_Funding_per_100000 ~ z_Citations, data = dat)
model_ols_glance<-glance(model_ols)
ols_table <- tbl_regression(model_ols,  intercept = TRUE) 

Citations_quad1 <- lm(z_Funding_per_100000 ~ z_Citations + z_Citations2, data = dat)
my_Citations_quad1<-tbl_regression(Citations_quad1,  intercept = TRUE, estimate_fun = ~ style_number(.x, digits = 4))
my_Citations_quad_glance1<-data.frame(glance(Citations_quad1))



Mod <- model_ols %>%
  tidy(conf.int = TRUE) %>%
  rename(Term = term, B = estimate, SE = std.error, Lower = conf.low, Upper = conf.high) %>%
  mutate(Term = ifelse(Term == "(Intercept)", "Intercept", Term)) %>% select(Term, B, Lower, Upper, p.value )



set.seed(172635)

WildObj <- wild.boot(z_Funding_per_100000 ~ z_Citations, data = dat, B = 10000) 

# Extract all bootstrap estimates,
B <- colMeans(WildObj$bootEstParam)  # Compute mean of bootstrap estimates

boot_ci_df <- data.frame(
  Term = colnames(WildObj$bootEstParam),  
  boot.B = colMeans(WildObj$bootEstParam),  
  boot.SE = apply(WildObj$bootEstParam, 2, sd),  
  boot.Lower = apply(WildObj$bootEstParam, 2, quantile, probs = 0.025),
  boot.Upper = apply(WildObj$bootEstParam, 2, quantile, probs = 0.975)
) %>% 
  mutate(Term = ifelse(Term == "(Intercept)", "Intercept", Term)) %>% 
  mutate(`p-value` = 2 * (1 - pnorm(abs(boot.B / boot.SE)))) %>% select(-boot.SE) 

boot_df <- as.data.frame(WildObj$bootEstParam)


orig_est <- as.numeric(WildObj$origEstParam)
names(orig_est) <- colnames(boot_df)

orig_df <- data.frame(
  Term = names(orig_est),
  Orig_Estimate = orig_est
)



boot.dist1 <- boot_df |>
  pivot_longer(cols = everything(), names_to = "Term", values_to = "Estimate") |>
  left_join(orig_df, by = "Term") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Estimate)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "steelblue", color = "white") +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = Orig_Estimate), color = "red", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~ Term, scales = "free_y", ncol = 1) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Wild Bootstrap Distributions of Coefficients",
    x = "Bootstrapped Estimates",
    y = "Frequency"
  )


ggsave("figures/boot.dist1.png", plot = boot.dist1, width = 8, height = 6, dpi = 300)



# Merge Type III SS OLS and Bootstrap results
comparison1 <- left_join(Mod, boot_ci_df, by = "Term") %>%
  mutate(
   # Compute absolute coefficient change
    A_B_change = (B - boot.B),
    A_B_pct_change =  pmax(pmin(round((A_B_change / abs(B)) * 100, 2), 1000),-1000),

    # Compute absolute changes in CI bounds
    A_lower_change = (Lower - boot.Lower),
    A_upper_change = (Upper - boot.Upper),

    # Compute percentage changes in CI bounds
    A_lower_pct_change = round((A_lower_change / abs(Lower)) * 100, 2),
    A_upper_pct_change =  pmax( pmin(round((A_upper_change / abs(Upper)) * 100, 2), 1000),-1000),

    # Compute OLS and Bootstrap CI widths
    A_range_ols = (Upper - Lower),  
    A_range_boot = (boot.Upper - boot.Lower),  
    A_range_change = (A_range_boot - A_range_ols),
    A_range_pct_change =  pmax( pmin(round((A_range_change / A_range_ols) * 100, 2), 1000),-1000),

    # Flag changes 
     B_Diff_0.1 = ifelse(abs(A_B_change) >= 0.1, "Yes", "No"),
    Lower_Diff_0.1 = ifelse(abs(A_lower_change) >= 0.1, "Yes", "No"),
    Upper_Diff_0.1 = ifelse(abs(A_upper_change) >= 0.1, "Yes", "No"),
    Range_Diff_0.1 = ifelse(abs(A_range_change) >= 0.1, "Yes", "No"),
    B_Diff_0.2 = ifelse(abs(A_B_change) >= 0.2, "Yes", "No"),
    Lower_Diff_0.2 = ifelse(abs(A_lower_change) >= 0.2, "Yes", "No"),
    Upper_Diff_0.2 = ifelse(abs(A_upper_change) >= 0.2, "Yes", "No"),
    Range_Diff_0.2 = ifelse(abs(A_range_change) >=0.2, "Yes", "No"),
    
    B_10pct = ifelse(abs(A_B_pct_change) >= 10, "Yes", "No"),
    Lower_10pct = ifelse(abs(A_lower_pct_change) >= 10, "Yes", "No"),
    Upper_10pct = ifelse(abs(A_upper_pct_change) >= 10, "Yes", "No"),
    Range_10pct = ifelse(abs(A_range_pct_change) >= 10, "Yes", "No"),
                changep = p.value - `p-value`,
        SigChange = case_when(
      p.value < 0.05 & `p-value` < 0.05 ~ "Remains significant",
      p.value >= 0.05 & `p-value` >= 0.05 ~ "Remains non-sig",
      p.value < 0.05 & `p-value` >= 0.05 ~ "Change from sig to non-sig",
      p.value >= 0.05 & `p-value` < 0.05 ~ "Change from non-sig to sig"),
    SigChangeDirection = case_when(
      p.value < 0.05 & `p-value` < 0.05 & (sign(B) != sign(boot.B)) ~ "Remains sig, B changes direction",
      p.value < 0.05 & `p-value` < 0.05 & (sign(B) == sign(boot.B)) ~ "Remains sig, B same direction",
      p.value>= 0.05 & `p-value` < 0.05 & (sign(B) == sign(boot.B) | sign(B) != sign(boot.B)) ~ "Non-sig to sig, B same direction",
      p.value < 0.05 & `p-value` >= 0.05 & (sign(B) != sign(boot.B) | sign(B) == sign(boot.B)) ~ "Sig to non-sig, B changes direction",
      p.value >= 0.05 & `p-value` >= 0.05 & (sign(B) != sign(boot.B)) ~ "Remains non-sig, B changes direction",
      p.value >= 0.05 & `p-value` >= 0.05 & (sign(B) == sign(boot.B)) ~ "Remains non-sig, B same direction",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_)) %>%
  select(
    Term,
    B, boot.B, A_B_change, A_B_pct_change, B_10pct, B_Diff_0.1, B_Diff_0.2,
    Lower, boot.Lower, A_lower_change, A_lower_pct_change, Lower_10pct, Lower_Diff_0.1, Lower_Diff_0.2,
    Upper, boot.Upper, A_upper_change, A_upper_pct_change, Upper_10pct, Upper_Diff_0.1, Upper_Diff_0.2,
    A_range_ols, A_range_boot, A_range_change, A_range_pct_change,  Range_10pct,
      Range_Diff_0.1, Range_Diff_0.2, p.value, "p-value", changep, SigChange, SigChangeDirection) 




beta1<- comparison1 %>% select(Term, B, boot.B, A_B_change, A_B_pct_change, B_10pct, B_Diff_0.1, B_Diff_0.2) %>%
   mutate(A_B_pct_change = as.numeric(A_B_pct_change), A_B_pct_change = case_when(A_B_pct_change > 10000 ~ NA_real_, TRUE ~ A_B_pct_change)) %>% flextable %>% autofit %>% 
set_header_labels(A_B_change = "B_diff", A_B_pct_change = "%_Diff",  B_10pct="Diff_10%" ,   B_Diff_0.1 = "Diff_0.1", B_Diff_0.2 = "Diff_0.2" ) %>%
              colformat_double(digits = thresround) %>%  
  flextable::add_footer_lines("B =  standardized regression coefficient reproduced B; boot.B =  boostrapped standardized reproduced B; B_diff = change in B - boot.B; %_Diff = percentage difference, percentage changes were truncated at ±1000%; Diff_10% =  difference ≥10% ; Diff_0.1 and Diff_0.2  =  absolute difference ≥0.1 and ≥0.2, respectively.") 

lower1<- comparison1 %>% select(Term, Lower, boot.Lower, A_lower_change, A_lower_pct_change, Lower_10pct, Lower_Diff_0.1, Lower_Diff_0.2) %>% flextable %>% autofit %>% 
set_header_labels(A_lower_change = "Lower_diff", A_lower_pct_change = "%_Diff", Lower_10pct = "Diff_10%",  Lower_Diff_0.1 = "Diff_0.1",  Lower_Diff_0.2 = "Diff_0.2") %>%
              colformat_double(digits = thresround)  %>%  
  flextable::add_footer_lines("Lower =  standardized reproduced lower CI; boot.Lower =  boostrapped standardized reproduced lower CI; Lower_diff = change in Lower - boot.Lower; %_change = percentage difference, percentage changes were truncated at ±1000%; Diff_10% =  difference ≥10% ; Diff_0.1 and Diff_0.2  =  absolute difference ≥0.1 and ≥0.2, respectively." ) 

upper1<- comparison1 %>% select(Term, Upper, boot.Upper, A_upper_change, A_upper_pct_change, Upper_10pct, Upper_Diff_0.1, Upper_Diff_0.2) %>% flextable %>% autofit %>% 
set_header_labels(A_upper_change = "Upper_diff", A_upper_pct_change = "%_Diff", Upper_10pct ="Diff_10%", Upper_Diff_0.1 = "Diff_0.1", Upper_Diff_0.2 = "Diff_0.2") %>%
              colformat_double(digits = thresround) %>%  
  flextable::add_footer_lines("Upper =  standardized reproduced upper CI; boot.Upper =  boostrapped standardized reproduced upper CI; Upper_diff = change in Upper - boot.Upper; %_change = percentage difference, percentage changes were truncated at ±1000%; Diff_10% =  difference ≥10% ; Diff_0.1 and Diff_0.2  =  absolute difference ≥0.1 and ≥0.2, respectively." ) 

range1<- comparison1 %>% select(Term, A_range_ols, A_range_boot, A_range_change, A_range_pct_change, Range_10pct, Range_Diff_0.1, Range_Diff_0.2) %>% flextable %>% autofit %>% 
set_header_labels(A_range_ols ="Range", A_range_boot="boot.Range", A_range_change = "Range_Diff" ,  A_range_pct_change="%_Diff", Range_10pct="Diff_10%", Range_Diff_0.1="Diff_0.1", Range_Diff_0.2="Diff_0.2") %>%
              colformat_double(digits = thresround) %>%  
  flextable::add_footer_lines("Range =  standardized reproduced CI range; boot.B =  boostrapped standardized reproduced CI range; Range_diff = change in CI Range ; %_change = percentage difference, Diff_10% =  difference ≥10% ; Diff_0.1 and Diff_0.2  =  absolute difference ≥0.1 and ≥0.2, respectively.") 



p.val1<- comparison1 %>% select(Term, p.value, "p-value", changep,SigChangeDirection) %>%
  mutate(p.value = sapply(p.value, round_p)) %>% mutate(`p-value` = sapply(`p-value`, round_p)) %>%flextable %>% autofit %>% set_header_labels(p.value ="p-value", "p-value"="boot.p-value") %>% 
              colformat_double(digits = thresround) %>%  
  flextable::add_footer_lines("p-value =  standardized reproduced p-value; boot.p-value =  boostrapped standardized reproduced p-value; changep = change in p-value - boot.p-value; SigChangeDirection =  statistical significance and B change between reproduced and bootstrapped model.") 


```

#### Bootstrapped results

Wild bootstrapping was performed with 10,000 iterations.

##### Change in regression coefficients

```{r, ft.align="left"}
beta1
```

##### Change in lower 95% confidence interval

```{r, ft.align="left"}

lower1

```

##### Change in upper 95% confidence interval

```{r, ft.align="left"}
upper1
```

##### Change in Range of 95% confidence interval

```{r, ft.align="left"}
range1

```

##### Change in p-value significance and regression coefficient direction

```{r, ft.align="left"}
p.val1
```

##### Check the distribution of bootstrap estimates

The bootstrap distribution of each coefficient appeared approximately normal and centered near the original estimate (red dashed line), suggesting that the estimates are relatively stable. No strong skewness or multimodality was observed.

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/boot.dist1.png')

```

#### Conclusions based on the bootstrapped model

This model was inferentially reproducible. While some statistics changed by 10% or more, these differences were not meaningful, with a change in standardized regression coefficients of less than 0.1. The direction of effects and statistical significance remained consistent between the reproduced and bootstrapped models.
:::

# Model 2

::: panel-tabset
### Original results

##### Model results for `r namey2`

```{r, ft.align="left"}
O_coefficients_flextable_2

```

##### Fit statistics for `r namey2`

```{r, ft.align="left"}
O_fit_stats_flextable_2

```

##### ANOVA table for `r namey2`

```{r,ft.align="left"}
O_anova_flextable_2
```

### Reproduced results

##### Model results `r namey2`

```{r , ft.align="left"}

R_coefficients_flextable_2
```

##### Model fit for `r namey2`

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_fit_stats_flextable_2
```

##### ANOVA table for `r namey2`

```{r, ft.align="left"}

R_anova_flextable_2
```

##### Visualisation of regression model

The blue line shows the best line of fit with shading representing 95% confidence intervals, while holding all other covariates constant. The dots show partial residuals, which reflect the observed data adjusted for all other predictors except the one being plotted.

```{r, out.width = "80%", out.height="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/reg_plot_2.png') 

```

### Differences

##### Forest plot showing Original and Reproduced coefficients and 95% confidence intervals for `r namey2`

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/forest_plot_model_2.png')

```

##### Change in regression coefficients

```{r, ft.align="left"}
CC_model_2
```

##### Change in lower 95% confidence intervals for coefficients

```{r, ft.align="left"}
CL_model_2

```

##### Change in upper 95% confidence intervals for coefficients

```{r, ft.align="left"}

CU_model_2

```

##### Change in R^2^

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R2_model_2
```

##### Change in p-values

```{r, ft.align="left"}
Cp_model_2
```

##### Bland Altman plot showing differences between original and reproduced p-values for `r namey2`

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/bland_altman_plot_model_2.png')

```

##### Results for p-values

Some p-values were reproduced, others were not.

#### Conclusion computational reproducibility

`r NotCompRep`

### Sensitivity analysis

`r  Nosens`
:::

# Model 3

::: panel-tabset
### Original results

##### Model results for `r namey3`

```{r,  ft.align="left"}

O_coefficients_flextable_3

```

##### Fit Statistics `r namey3`

```{r,  ft.align="left"}
O_fit_stats_flextable_3
```

##### ANOVA Table for `r namey3`

```{r,  ft.align="left"}
O_anova_flextable_3

```

### Reproduced results

##### Model results for `r namey3`

```{r,  ft.align="left"}
R_coefficients_flextable_3
```

##### Fit statistics for `r namey3`

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_fit_stats_flextable_3
```

##### ANOVA Table for `r namey3`

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_anova_flextable_3
```

##### Visualisation of regression model

The blue line shows the best line of fit with shading representing 95% confidence intervals, while holding all other covariates constant. The dots show partial residuals, which reflect the observed data adjusted for all other predictors except the one being plotted.

```{r, out.width = "80%", out.height="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/reg_plot_3.png') 

```

##### Checking residuals plots for patterns

Blue line showing quadratic fit for residuals

```{r, out.width = "100%", out.height="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/res_plot_3.png') 

```

##### Testing residuals for non linear relationships

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_residuals_flextable_3
```

##### Checking univariate relationships with the dependent variable using scatterplots

Blue line shows linear relationship, red line indicates relationship inferred by GAM modelling

```{r, out.width = "80%", out.height="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/scatter_plot_3.png') 

```

##### Linearity results

`r linno`

##### Testing for homoscedasticity

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_homoscedasticity_flextable_3
```

##### Homoscedasticity results

-   `r ifelse(R_homoscedasticity_raw_df_3$p.value[1] < 0.05, "The studentized Breusch-Pagan test indicates heteroscedasticity.", "The studentized Breusch-Pagan test supports homoscedasticity.")`
-   `r somehet`

##### Model descriptives including cook's distance and leverage to understand outliers

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_description_flextable_3

```

##### Cooks threshold

Cook's distance measures the overall change in fit, if the *ith* observation is removed. Potentially influential observations are identified by $\text{Cook's Distance}_i > \frac{4}{n}$, where *n* is the number of observations. In practice, a threshold of 0.5 to 1 is often used to identify influential observations.

```{r, out.width = "80%", out.height="80%"}

knitr::include_graphics('figures/cooks_threshold_3.png')
```

##### DFFIT threshold

DFFIT measures how many standard deviations the fitted values will change when the *ith* observation is removed. Potential influential observations $\left| \text{DFFITS}_i \right| > \frac{2\sqrt{p}}{\sqrt{n}}$ where *p* is the number of predictors (including the intercept), and *n* is the number of observations. In practice, this can result in a large number of points identified, a practical cut-off of 1 was used to flag observations with meaningful impact.

```{r, out.width = "80%", out.height="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/dffit_threshold_3.png')

```

##### DFBETA threshold

DFBETAS quantify the influence of the *ith* observation on the *jth* regression coefficient as the change in that coefficient when the observation is omitted, expressed in units of the coefficient’s estimated standard error. There is a DFBETA for each model parameter. Potential influential observations $|\text{DFBETA}_{ij}| > \frac{2}{\sqrt{n}}$, where *n* is the number of observations. In larger datasets, this threshold can flag a high number of observations with only minor influence on the model. A practical cut-off of 1 was used to flag observations with meaningful impact.

```{r, out.width = "100%", out.height="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/dbetas_threshold_3.png') 

```

##### Influence plot

Observations with high leverage (horizontal) and large residuals (vertical, typically at ±2 or ±3 studentized residuals) are concerning, as they may disproportionately influence the model. This combination is reflected by large bubbles with high Cook's distance indicated by darker shadings of blue.

```{r, out.width = "100%", out.height="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/cooks_plots_3.png') 

```

##### COVRATIO plot

COVRATIO measures the overall change in the precision (covariance matrix) of the estimated regression coefficients when the ith observation is removed. Values close to 1 indicate little influence on the model’s precision. Values below 1 suggest that an observation inflates the variances and reduces precision, resulting in wider confidence intervals, whereas values above 1 suggest deflated variances and narrower confidence intervals. A commonly cited guideline is $\left|\mathrm{COVRATIO}_i - 1\right| > \frac{3p}{n}$, where *p* is the number of parameters and *n* is the number of observations. A practical cut-off between 0.9 to 1.1 was used to flag observations with meaningful impact on precision, although there is no agreed universal alternative cut-off.

```{r, out.width = "100%", out.height="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/covratio_plot_3.png') 

```

##### Observations of interest identified by the influence plot

```{r, ft.align="left"}

 R_cooks_distance_flextable_3
```

##### Results for outliers and influential points

Two observations had studentized residuals near 3. One of these observations was an inflential outlier with Cook’s distance of approximately 4, with DFBETAS and DFFITS also outside conventional ranges. COVRATIO values substantially \< 1, suggesting the observation may effect point estimates but potential inflation of standard errors (wider confidence intervals).

##### Checking for normality of the residuals using a Q–Q plot

```{r, out.width = "80%", out.height="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/qq_plot_3.png') 
```

##### Normality of residuals using Shapiro-Wilk and Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_normality_ks_flextable_3
R_normality_sw_flextable_3
```

##### Normality results

-   `r ifelse(R_normality_ks_raw_df_3$p.value[1] < 0.05, "The Kolmogorov-Smirnov test indicates residuals may not be normally distributed.", "The Kolmogorov-Smirnov supports residuals being normally distributed.")`
-   `r ifelse(R_normality_sw_raw_df_3$p.value[1] < 0.05, "The Shapiro-Wilk normality test indicates residuals may not be normally distributed.", "The Shapiro-Wilk supports residuals being normally distributed.")`
-   `r normalQ`

##### Assessing collinearity with VIF

```{r,  ft.align="left"}
R_collinearity_flextable_3
```

##### Collinearity results

-   `r as.character(printvif(R_collinearity_raw_df_3$vif))` <br>
-   `r vifnoprob`

##### Assessing independence with the Durbin–Watson test for autocorrelation

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R_independence_flextable_3
```

##### Independence results

-   `r ifelse(R_independence_raw_df_3$p[1] < 0.05, "The Durbin–Watson test suggests there are auto-correlation issues.", "The Durbin–Watson test suggests there are no auto-correlation issues.")`
-   `r indDesYes`

##### Assumption conclusions

No meaningful departures from the assumptions of normality or independence were observed. However, residual plots and diagnostic tests indicated potential issues with linearity, suggesting that a non-linear relationship may not have been adequately captured, and residuals may be mildly heteroscedastic. Additionally, the presence of observations with high DFBETA, DFFIT and COVRatio indicates that further investigation of potential influential points is warranted.

### Differences

##### Forest plot showing original and reproduced coefficients and 95% confidence intervals for `r namey3`

```{r}

knitr::include_graphics('figures/forest_plot_model_3.png')

```

##### Change in regression coefficients

```{r, ft.align="left"}
CC_model_3
```

##### Change in lower 95% confidence intervals for coefficients

```{r, ft.align="left"}

CL_model_3
```

##### Change in upper 95% confidence intervals for coefficients

```{r, ft.align="left"}

CU_model_3
```

##### Change in R^2^

```{r, ft.align="left"}
R2_model_3

```

##### Change in p-values

```{r, ft.align="left"}

Cp_model_3
```

##### Bland Altman Plot showing differences between original and reproduced p-values for `r namey3`

```{r}

knitr::include_graphics('figures/bland_altman_plot_model_3.png')

```

##### Results for p-values

P-values were reproduced with all differences close to zero.

#### Conclusion computational reproducibility

`r MostCompRep`

### Sensitivity analysis

#### Methods

The model was successfully reproduced; however, residual diagnostics indicated potential heteroscedasticity, nonlinearity, and observations that may influence point estimates or confidence-interval widths. Evidence of non-linearity was primarily associated with GDP. To assess the impact of influential observations, a sensitivity analysis was conducted in which all continuous variables were standardized, and models were fitted with and without the influential observation to evaluate changes in effect size.

To further examine departures from linearity, models fitted on the original scale were visualised by comparing linear, quadratic, and cubic specifications, both with and without the influential observation. As the apparent polynomial relationship was driven by the influential observation, an additional sensitivity analysis was undertaken in which both the outcome and GDP were log-transformed to assess whether this specification improved linearity and model assumptions.

#### Results

This model was not inferentially reproducible with outliers and linearity issue substantially changing the results.

```{r}

dat2<-dat %>% filter(Country != "Luxembourg")

dat2 <- dat2 %>%  
  mutate(across(c(Funding_per_100000, DALY_per_100000, Citations, Average_GDP_PC), ~ as.numeric(scale(.)), .names = "z_{.col}"))  %>%  mutate(z_Citations2=z_Citations^2)%>%
 mutate(z_Average_GDP_PC2=z_Average_GDP_PC^2) %>%
   mutate(z_Average_GDP_PC3=z_Average_GDP_PC^3)

model_ols3 <- lm(z_Funding_per_100000 ~ z_Citations + z_Average_GDP_PC + population_quartile, data = dat)
model_ols_glance3<-glance(model_ols3)
my_model_ols3 <- tbl_regression(model_ols3,  intercept = TRUE) %>% add_n
my_modelols3 <- tbl_regression(model_ols3,  intercept = TRUE) 

model_ols_outRemoved3 <- lm(z_Funding_per_100000 ~ z_Citations + z_Average_GDP_PC + population_quartile, data = dat2)
my_model_ols_outRemoved3 <- tbl_regression(model_ols_outRemoved3,  intercept = TRUE) %>% add_n


Outlier_comparison <- tbl_merge(
  tbls = list(my_model_ols3 , my_model_ols_outRemoved3),
  tab_spanner = c("All_data", "Outlier Removed")) 









```

##### Standardized z_Funding_per_100000 showing with and without outlier

The authors identified an outlying value for Average_GDP_PC corresponding to Luxembourg; however, this observation was retained in the analysis without a sensitivity assessment. Removal of this observation resulted in substantial changes in coefficient estimates, with GDP more than doubling without the outlier, consistent with being an influential point identified by a large Cook’s distance. Although it is generally appropriate not to exclude outliers without strong justification, influential observations should be explicitly examined through sensitivity analyses to assess their impact on model estimates and inference.

```{r}
Outlier_comparison
```

##### Comparison of Funding_per_100000 with non-linear terms for Average_GDP_PC with outlier included

It can be seen that linear, quadratic and cubic models do not fit the data well, all models are heavily impacted by the outlier.

```{r}

## ---- ensure factor ----
dat$population_quartile <- as.factor(dat$population_quartile)

## ---- models: linear, quadratic, cubic (GDP) ----
model_lin <- lm(
  Funding_per_100000 ~ Citations +
    Average_GDP_PC +
    population_quartile,
  data = dat
)

model_quad <- lm(
  Funding_per_100000 ~ Citations +
    Average_GDP_PC + I(Average_GDP_PC^2) +
    population_quartile,
  data = dat
)

model_cubic <- lm(
  Funding_per_100000 ~ Citations +
    Average_GDP_PC + I(Average_GDP_PC^2) + I(Average_GDP_PC^3) +
    population_quartile,
  data = dat
)

## ---- choose reference population quartile (most common) ----
pop_ref <- "1st quartile"

## ---- prediction grid: GDP varies, others fixed ----
cit_mean <- mean(dat$Citations, na.rm = TRUE)

newdat <- data.frame(
  Average_GDP_PC = seq(
    min(dat$Average_GDP_PC, na.rm = TRUE),
    max(dat$Average_GDP_PC, na.rm = TRUE),
    length.out = 100
  ),
  Citations = cit_mean,
  population_quartile = factor(
    pop_ref,
    levels = levels(dat$population_quartile)
  )
)

## ---- predictions ----
pred_lin   <- predict(model_lin,   newdata = newdat)
pred_quad  <- predict(model_quad,  newdata = newdat)
pred_cubic <- predict(model_cubic, newdata = newdat)

plotdat <- newdat |>
  mutate(
    fit_lin   = pred_lin,
    fit_quad  = pred_quad,
    fit_cubic = pred_cubic
  )

## ---- plot: GDP effect with linear, quadratic, cubic fits ----
p <- ggplot() +
  ## observed data
  geom_point(
    data = dat,
    aes(x = Average_GDP_PC, y = Funding_per_100000),
    alpha = 0.4,
    size = 2
  ) +
  ## linear
  geom_line(
    data = plotdat,
    aes(x = Average_GDP_PC, y = fit_lin),
    linewidth = 1,
    colour = "black",
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
  ## quadratic
  geom_line(
    data = plotdat,
    aes(x = Average_GDP_PC, y = fit_quad),
    linewidth = 1,
    colour = "blue",
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
  ## cubic
  geom_line(
    data = plotdat,
    aes(x = Average_GDP_PC, y = fit_cubic),
    linewidth = 1,
      colour = "darkorange",
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Average GDP per capita",
    y = "Funding per 100,000",
    title = "Adjusted association between GDP per capita and funding",
    subtitle = paste0(
    "Citations held at mean; ",
    "population quartile = ", pop_ref,
    "\nBlack = linear, Blue = quadratic, Orange = cubic"
  )
) +
  theme_minimal()

print(p)









```

##### Comparison of Funding_per_100000 with non-linear terms for Average_GDP_PC without outlier included

After excluding the influential observation, a quadratic association between funding and GDP was evident. Although this sensitivity analysis is informative for understanding the underlying functional form, the observation should be retained in the primary analysis and modelled appropriately. Given that a single observation can exert disproportionate influence, the data were log-transformed to stabilise the variance and reduce the impact of extreme values.

```{r}
#without oulier

## ---- ensure factor ----
dat2$population_quartile <- as.factor(dat2$population_quartile)

## ---- models: linear, quadratic, cubic (GDP) ----
model_lin <- lm(
  Funding_per_100000 ~ Citations +
    Average_GDP_PC +
    population_quartile,
  data = dat2
)

model_quad <- lm(
  Funding_per_100000 ~ Citations +
    Average_GDP_PC + I(Average_GDP_PC^2) +
    population_quartile,
  data = dat2
)

model_cubic <- lm(
  Funding_per_100000 ~ Citations +
    Average_GDP_PC + I(Average_GDP_PC^2) + I(Average_GDP_PC^3) +
    population_quartile,
  data = dat2
)

## ---- choose reference population quartile (most common) ----
pop_ref <- "1st quartile"
## ---- prediction grid: GDP varies, others fixed ----
cit_mean <- mean(dat2$Citations, na.rm = TRUE)

newdat <- data.frame(
  Average_GDP_PC = seq(
    min(dat2$Average_GDP_PC, na.rm = TRUE),
    max(dat2$Average_GDP_PC, na.rm = TRUE),
    length.out = 100
  ),
  Citations = cit_mean,
  population_quartile = factor(
    pop_ref,
    levels = levels(dat2$population_quartile)
  )
)

## ---- predictions ----
pred_lin   <- predict(model_lin,   newdata = newdat)
pred_quad  <- predict(model_quad,  newdata = newdat)
pred_cubic <- predict(model_cubic, newdata = newdat)

plotdat <- newdat |>
  mutate(
    fit_lin   = pred_lin,
    fit_quad  = pred_quad,
    fit_cubic = pred_cubic
  )

## ---- plot: GDP effect with linear, quadratic, cubic fits ----
p <- ggplot() +
  ## observed data
  geom_point(
    data = dat2,
    aes(x = Average_GDP_PC, y = Funding_per_100000),
    alpha = 0.4,
    size = 2
  ) +
  ## linear
  geom_line(
    data = plotdat,
    aes(x = Average_GDP_PC, y = fit_lin),
    linewidth = 1,
    colour = "black",
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
  ## quadratic
  geom_line(
    data = plotdat,
    aes(x = Average_GDP_PC, y = fit_quad),
    linewidth = 1,
    colour = "blue",
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
  ## cubic
  geom_line(
    data = plotdat,
    aes(x = Average_GDP_PC, y = fit_cubic),
    linewidth = 1,
    colour = "darkorange",
    linetype = "solid"
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Average GDP per capita",
    y = "Funding per 100,000",
    title = "Adjusted association between GDP per capita and funding",
    subtitle = paste0(
    "Citations held at mean; ",
    "population quartile = ", pop_ref,
    "\nBlack = linear, Blue = quadratic, Orange = cubic"
  )
) +
  theme_minimal()

print(p)








```

```{r}
library(gtsummary)
library(broom)
library(dplyr)

model_log_ols3 <- lm(
  log_Funding_per_100000 ~ Citations + log_Average_GDP_PC + population_quartile,
  data = dat
)

tidy_exp_lm <- function(x, ...) {
  broom::tidy(x, ...) |>
    mutate(
      estimate = exp(estimate),
      conf.low = exp(conf.low),
      conf.high = exp(conf.high)
    )
}

tbl <- tbl_regression(
  model_log_ols3,
  intercept = TRUE,
  tidy_fun = tidy_exp_lm,
  estimate_fun = ~ style_number(.x, digits = 3)
) |>
  modify_header(estimate ~ "**exp(β)**") #|>
 # modify_header(conf.low ~ "**Lower 95% CI**", conf.high ~ "**Upper 95% CI**")

```

To mitigate the influence of an extreme observation, funding and GDP per capita were log-transformed, while citations were retained on the original scale.

##### Back transformed coefficients

```{r}
tbl
```

Each additional citation was associated with an 8.2% increase in funding (95% CI: 3.7% to 12.9%, p \< 0.001). There were no statistically significant differences in funding between population quartiles. For example, countries in the second population quartile had 1.2 times the funding as those in the first quartile (95% CI: 0.68 to 2.18, p = 0.501).

The exponentiated coefficient for log(GDP) (3.3) represents the change in funding associated with an approximately 2.7-fold increase in GDP (because natural logarithms are used) and is therefore difficult to interpret directly. Because both funding and GDP were log-transformed, the coefficient for log(GDP) represents an elasticity. The coefficient (1.194; see table below) indicates that funding is proportional to GDP raised to the power of 1.194, meaning funding increases with GDP according to this power relationship. For example, a 10% increase in GDP corresponds to an estimated 12% increase in funding (1.10^1.194^ - 1).

As the model was fitted on the log scale, fit statistics (e.g. R^2^, AIC, BIC) are not directly comparable with those from models on the original scale. The focus was therefore on obtaining a valid inferential model, assessed through residual diagnostics to ensure that the log transformation improved adherence to model assumptions and mitigated the impact of outliers. Although the influential observation continued to have some impact, its influence was substantially reduced, with Cook’s distance below 0.5. On the log scale, Residual diagnostics did not indicate evidence of non-linearity between GDP per capita and funding.

```{r, results='hide'}
my_res_file <- "figures/residualPlots_model_log_ols3.png"

png(my_res_file, width = 1200, height = 800, res = 150)
car::residualPlots(model_log_ols3, type = "rstandard")
dev.off()
  
```

**Residual plots**

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics('figures/residualPlots_model_log_ols3.png')
```

```{r}
cooks_plot <- influencePlot(model_log_ols3, xlab = "Leverage", ylab = "Studentized Residuals")
```

Residual diagnostics indicated mild heteroscedasticity. As a sensitivity analysis, heteroscedasticity-robust standard errors were applied, leading to inflated standard errors relative to the conventional OLS estimates and was therefore appropriate.

\newline

##### Comparison of OLS and robust standard errors with log coefficients

```{r}


#--- helper: tidy OLS with classical CI ---
tidy_lm_ci <- function(model) {
  tidy(model, conf.int = TRUE) |>
    select(term, estimate, std.error, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) |>
  mutate(across(c(estimate, std.error, conf.low, conf.high),~ round(.x, 3)))
}

#--- helper: tidy robust (HC3) with Wald CI ---
tidy_RSE_ci <- function(model, vcov_type = "HC3") {
  tidy(coeftest(model, vcov = vcovHC(model, type = vcov_type))) |>
    mutate(
      conf.low  = estimate - qnorm(0.975) * std.error,
      conf.high = estimate + qnorm(0.975) * std.error
    ) |>
    select(term, estimate, std.error, conf.low, conf.high, p.value)|>
  mutate(across(c(estimate, std.error, conf.low, conf.high),~ round(.x, 3)))
}

#--- build side-by-side table; keep OLS names, suffix robust with _RSE ---
tab_side_by_side <-
  tidy_lm_ci(model_log_ols3) |>
  mutate(p.value = round_p(p.value)) |>
  left_join(
    tidy_RSE_ci(model_log_ols3, vcov_type = "HC3") |>
      mutate(p.value = round_p(p.value)) |>
      rename_with(~ paste0(.x, "_RSE"), -term),
    by = "term") 

#--- flextable with grouped headers ---
ft3 <-
  flextable(tab_side_by_side) |>
  set_header_labels(
    term = "Variable",
    estimate = "Estimate",
    std.error = "SE",
    conf.low = "Lower",
    conf.high = "Upper",
    p.value = "p-value",
    estimate_RSE = "Estimate",
    std.error_RSE = "SE",
    conf.low_RSE = "Lower",
    conf.high_RSE = "Upper",
    p.value_RSE = "p-value"
  ) |>
  add_header_row(
    values = c("",
               "Ordinary least squares", "Robust SE (HC3)"),
    colwidths = c(1, 5, 5)
  ) |>
  flextable::align(j = 1, align = "left", part = "all") |>
  flextable::align(j = 2:ncol(tab_side_by_side), align = "left", part = "all") |>
  autofit()

ft3
```
:::

```{r}
Assumptions_data<- bind_rows(M1Rassump,M3Rassump) 
comparison1<-comparison1 %>% mutate(Model=1, paper_ID="32")
write.csv(comparison1, file="data/boot_32.csv")
write.csv(repdat, file="data/repdat_32.csv")
write.csv(Assumptions_data,file="data/Assumptions_data32.csv")
write.csv(fdat,file="data/model_data32.csv")
```
